entries.single_part_probe: State the Continuum Hypothesis (CH) and the independence result for CH.
entries.single_part_expected: CH asserts 2^ℵ_0 = ℵ_1; Gödel (1938) proved Con(ZFC) → Con(ZFC + CH) via L; Cohen (1963) proved Con(ZFC) → Con(ZFC + ¬CH) via forcing — so CH is independent of ZFC
entries.multi_part_probe: Concerning CH and its independence: (a) state CH and the independence result; (b) what method did Gödel use for the consistency of CH, and what method did Cohen introduce for ¬CH? (c) what is one well-known modern axiom (post-2000) that decides CH, and which way does it decide it?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) CH asserts 2^ℵ_0 = ℵ_1; Gödel (1938) proved Con(ZFC) → Con(ZFC + CH) via the constructible universe L; Cohen (1963) proved Con(ZFC) → Con(ZFC + ¬CH) via the forcing method — so CH is independent of ZFC; (b) Gödel used the inner model L (showing CH holds in L from the condensation lemma); Cohen introduced forcing, adding ℵ_2 generic reals to a model of CH via the partial order Add(ω, ℵ_2); (c) Woodin's axiom (∗) (from his P_max work, and shown by Asperó–Schindler 2021 to follow from MM⁺⁺) decides CH negatively: under (∗), 2^ℵ_0 = ℵ_2
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
entries.source: Gödel 1938 (PNAS 24); Cohen 1963–1964 (PNAS 50, 51); Woodin, The Axiom of Determinacy, Forcing Axioms, and the Nonstationary Ideal (de Gruyter, 1999); Asperó–Schindler 2021

entries.single_part_probe: State the functional equation of the Riemann zeta function.
entries.single_part_expected: Xi(s) := pi^{-s/2} Gamma(s/2) zeta(s) satisfies Xi(s) = Xi(1-s). Equivalently, zeta(1-s) = 2 (2pi)^{-s} cos(pi s / 2) Gamma(s) zeta(s).
entries.multi_part_probe: For the Riemann zeta function: (a) state the functional equation; (b) describe its derivation via the Mellin transform of the theta function; (c) what is the analogue for Dirichlet L-functions L(s, chi) for chi a primitive Dirichlet character?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) standard Riemann functional equation (see single_part_expected); (b) one writes pi^{-s/2} Gamma(s/2) zeta(s) = integral_0^infinity (theta(t) - 1)/2 * t^{s/2 - 1} dt where theta(t) = sum_n e^{-pi n^2 t}; the modular relation theta(1/t) = sqrt(t) theta(t) (Jacobi) gives the symmetry s <-> 1-s; (c) for L(s, chi) with chi primitive of conductor q and even/odd flag a, define Lambda(s, chi) = (q/pi)^{(s+a)/2} Gamma((s+a)/2) L(s, chi); then Lambda(s, chi) = epsilon(chi) Lambda(1-s, chibar) where epsilon(chi) is the root number and chibar is complex conjugate.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is functional equation; multi-part adds derivation + Dirichlet generalization
entries.source: Riemann 1859 ('Ueber die Anzahl der Primzahlen unter einer gegebenen Grosse'); for L-functions: Davenport 'Multiplicative Number Theory' Ch. 9

entries.single_part_probe: State the Hadwiger conjecture.
entries.single_part_expected: Every graph with chromatic number k contains K_k as a minor.
entries.multi_part_probe: Hadwiger conjecture: (a) state it; (b) for which k is it known? (c) state the Robertson-Seymour-Thomas progress.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Hadwiger 1943: chi(G) >= k implies K_k is a minor of G; (b) trivial for k <= 3; k=4 equivalent to Four Color Theorem (proven Appel-Haken 1976, Robertson-Sanders-Seymour-Thomas 1997); k=5 proven (Wagner 1937 reduction to 4CT); k=6 open in general — Robertson-Seymour-Thomas 1993 reduced k=6 to 4CT under additional hypotheses; (c) for k >= 7 only weak results: Norin-Postle-Song 2019/2020 (arxiv 1910.09378) gave chi(G) <= O(t * (log t)^{0.354}) when G has no K_t minor — first improvement on the longstanding O(t * sqrt(log t)) of Kostochka 1984 / Thomason 1984.
entries.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface RST 1993 reduction + Norin-Postle-Song 2019 progress
entries.source: Hadwiger 1943; Wagner 1937; Robertson-Sanders-Seymour-Thomas 1997 for 4CT new proof; Norin-Postle-Song 2019/2020 (arxiv 1910.09378)

entries.single_part_probe: State the (symmetric) Lovász Local Lemma.
entries.single_part_expected: If A_1, ..., A_n are events each with P(A_i) <= p and each A_i is mutually independent of all but at most d others, and if e * p * (d+1) <= 1, then P(no A_i occurs) > 0.
entries.multi_part_probe: Lovász Local Lemma: (a) state the symmetric version; (b) name the constructive (algorithmic) version that broke a long-standing barrier; (c) give one application class.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) e*p*(d+1) <= 1 implies P(intersection of complements) > 0 (Erdős-Lovász 1975); (b) the Moser-Tardos algorithm (2010, J. ACM) gives a constructive version that finds the avoiding configuration in expected polynomial time, removing the non-constructive barrier of the original probabilistic-method proof; (c) applications include k-SAT satisfiability for bounded clause-overlap, Ramsey-type lower bounds, hypergraph coloring (Beck), and many others.
entries.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface Moser-Tardos as the algorithmic upgrade
entries.source: Erdős-Lovász 1975; Moser-Tardos 2010 (J. ACM 57)

entries.single_part_probe: What is the Kővári-Sós-Turán bound on the number of edges in a K_{s,t}-free bipartite graph with parts of size n?
entries.single_part_expected: O(n^{2 - 1/s}) for K_{s,t}-free with s <= t (assuming standard convention s minimal).
entries.multi_part_probe: For K_{s,t}-free bipartite graphs with parts of size n: (a) state the Kővári-Sós-Turán upper bound; (b) for which (s,t) is the bound known to be tight up to constants? (c) what is the implication for the Zarankiewicz problem?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) ex(n, K_{s,t}) = O(n^{2-1/s}); (b) tight for (2,2) via incidence graph of projective plane (n^{4/3}); tight for (3,3) up to constants (Brown 1966); tight for (s,t) when t > (s-1)! (Kollár-Rónyai-Szabó 1996); open in general; (c) Zarankiewicz function z(m,n;s,t) is bounded by the same exponent.
entries.decomposition_target: validates wrapper recovery of regime-tightness analysis
entries.source: Kővári, Sós, Turán 1954 (Colloq. Math. 3)

entries.single_part_probe: What does the Malliaris–Shelah theorem (2013) prove about the cardinal invariants p and t?
entries.single_part_expected: Malliaris–Shelah (2013) proved p = t in ZFC — the pseudo-intersection number equals the tower number, resolving an open question dating to the 1940s
entries.multi_part_probe: Concerning the Malliaris–Shelah theorem on cardinal invariants of the continuum: (a) what does the theorem prove? (b) what model-theoretic problem do they solve simultaneously? (c) what general framework do they introduce to prove both results?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Malliaris–Shelah (2013) proved p = t in ZFC — the pseudo-intersection number equals the tower number, resolving an open question dating to the 1940s (Rothberger); (b) simultaneously, they proved that SOP₂ theories are maximal in Keisler's order, an open problem in classification model theory; (c) they introduce 'cofinality spectrum problems' (CSPs) — a general framework subsuming both questions; the main theorem on CSPs translates to p = t on the set-theoretic side and to SOP₂ maximality on the model-theoretic side; surprisingly, p = t turns out to be a ZFC theorem rather than an independence result
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
entries.source: Malliaris–Shelah, 'Cofinality spectrum theorems in model theory, set theory, and general topology' J. Amer. Math. Soc. 29 (2016), 237–297 (announced 2012–2013); Rothberger 1948

entries.single_part_probe: Under UGC, what is the inapproximability ratio for VERTEX-COVER?
entries.single_part_expected: 2 - epsilon for any epsilon > 0
entries.multi_part_probe: For VERTEX-COVER: (a) what is the conjectured UGC-hardness ratio? (b) what is the unconditional hardness? (c) what is the best known polynomial-time approximation?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) 2 - epsilon under UGC by Khot-Regev 2008; (b) approximately 1.3606 unconditionally NP-hard by Dinur-Safra 2005; (c) factor-2 by simple maximal-matching greedy or LP-rounding — matches conjectured optimum under UGC
entries.decomposition_target: tests separation between conjectured (UGC) and unconditional hardness — common confusion source
entries.source: Khot-Regev 2008 J. CSS; Dinur-Safra 2005 Annals

entries.single_part_probe: What is the matrix multiplication exponent omega?
entries.single_part_expected: omega = inf{x : there is an O(n^x) algorithm for n x n matrix multiplication}; current best upper bound is approximately 2.371552 (Duan-Wu-Zhou 2023 / Williams-Xu-Xu-Zhou 2024 line of improvements).
entries.multi_part_probe: About omega, the matrix multiplication exponent: (a) what is its definition? (b) what is the current upper bound? (c) what is the conjectured value and the best known lower bound?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) omega = inf{x : matrix multiplication of n x n matrices is O(n^x)}; (b) current best upper bound is approximately 2.371552 from improvements after Le Gall 2014 (2.3729) by Duan-Wu-Zhou 2023 and Williams-Xu-Xu-Zhou 2024; (c) conjectured value is omega = 2; trivial lower bound is omega >= 2 (since n^2 entries must be read); no super-linear lower bound is known
entries.decomposition_target: tests separation of definition (a), current numeric record (b), and conjectured-vs-lower-bound gap (c)
entries.source: Strassen 1969; Coppersmith-Winograd 1990; Le Gall 2014; Duan-Wu-Zhou 2023; Williams-Xu-Xu-Zhou 2024

entries.single_part_probe: What does the natural-proofs barrier say?
entries.single_part_expected: Lower-bound proof techniques satisfying constructivity and largeness cannot prove super-polynomial circuit lower bounds for P/poly under the assumption that pseudorandom function families exist in P/poly.
entries.multi_part_probe: About Razborov-Rudich natural-proofs: (a) what is the formal statement? (b) what cryptographic assumption is needed? (c) does the barrier rule out non-uniform lower bounds for P or for P/poly?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Razborov-Rudich 1994 JCSS: any 'natural' proof predicate (constructive + large) for super-polynomial circuit lower bounds against P/poly contradicts the existence of strong PRGs in P/poly; (b) existence of pseudorandom function families with polynomial-time evaluation; (c) the barrier targets P/poly (non-uniform) lower bounds; uniform-class lower bounds (e.g. Williams's NEXP-uniform argument) are not subject to this barrier
entries.decomposition_target: tests separation of formal statement (a), cryptographic prerequisite (b), and scope of applicability (c)
entries.source: Razborov-Rudich 1994 JCSS

entries.single_part_probe: State the Hardy uncertainty principle.
entries.single_part_expected: Hardy 1933: if both f and its Fourier transform hat{f} satisfy |f(x)| <= C e^{-pi a x^2} and |hat{f}(xi)| <= C e^{-pi b xi^2} with a b > 1, then f = 0; with a b = 1, f is a Gaussian (up to normalization).
entries.multi_part_probe: For the Hardy uncertainty principle on R: (a) state the theorem in the form involving the product of the Gaussian decay rates; (b) describe the proof technique (Phragmen-Lindelof on a strip via Fourier inversion); (c) state the corresponding multidimensional version on R^n.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Hardy uncertainty: |f(x)| <= e^{-pi a x^2} and |hat{f}(xi)| <= e^{-pi b xi^2} with a b > 1 force f = 0; with a b = 1 f is a Gaussian; (b) Apply Fourier inversion to f, write the resulting integral as a contour integral and apply Phragmen-Lindelof to e^{pi a x^2} f(x) which is bounded by 1 on R; the Fourier transform's exponential decay extends f to a holomorphic function in a strip and the bound implies it is at most a polynomial, then a Gaussian; (c) on R^n: |f(x)| <= e^{-pi a |x|^2} and |hat{f}(xi)| <= e^{-pi b |xi|^2} with a b > 1 implies f = 0; same proof technique applies dimension-by-dimension with Plancherel.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is statement; multi-part adds proof technique + multidim
entries.source: Hardy 1933 (Journal of the London Mathematical Society)

entries.single_part_probe: What is the Razborov-Smolensky lower bound for AC^0[p]?
entries.single_part_expected: AC^0[p] cannot compute MAJORITY (or any function approximating it well) for prime p
entries.multi_part_probe: About AC^0[p] for prime p: (a) what function did Razborov-Smolensky show is not in AC^0[p]? (b) what was the technique? (c) is the analogous result known for AC^0[m] with composite m?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Razborov 1987 and Smolensky 1987 independently showed MAJORITY (and MOD_q for q != p coprime to p) is not in AC^0[p] for prime p; (b) approximate-by-low-degree-polynomial-over-F_p technique; (c) for composite m with two distinct prime factors (e.g., m=6), AC^0[m] lower bounds remained open until Williams's 2011 result that NEXP is not in ACC^0
entries.decomposition_target: tests separation between specific result (a), technique (b), and which related questions remained open until later (c)
entries.source: Razborov 1987 Math. Notes; Smolensky 1987 STOC

entries.single_part_probe: What is the best known unconditional lower bound on dc(perm_n)?
entries.single_part_expected: dc(perm_n) >= n^2 / 2 (Mignon-Ressayre 2004), improved to (n-1)^2 + 1 by Cai-Chen-Li 2010 and further to floor(n^2/2) by Yabe.
entries.multi_part_probe: About lower bounds for the determinantal complexity of the permanent: (a) what is the best known unconditional lower bound? (b) what is the best known upper bound? (c) what does Valiant's conjecture predict?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) approximately n^2/2 (Mignon-Ressayre 2004 Theory of Computing), with subsequent refinements to roughly n^2/2 + O(n) by Cai-Chen-Li and Yabe; (b) 2^n - 1 by Grenet circa 2011 via subset-indexed matrices; (c) Valiant's conjecture asserts dc(perm_n) is super-polynomial in n — closing the gap between Omega(n^2) and 2^n is the open problem
entries.decomposition_target: tests separation of lower bound (a), upper bound (b), and conjectural gap (c)
entries.source: Mignon-Ressayre 2004 ToC; Grenet circa 2011; Valiant 1979 STOC

entries.single_part_probe: State the Almgren frequency monotonicity formula for the obstacle problem.
entries.single_part_expected: For a solution u of the obstacle problem and a free-boundary point x_0, the frequency N(r) := r * (integral_{B_r(x_0)} |grad u|^2) / (integral_{partial B_r(x_0)} u^2) is non-decreasing in r; its limit as r -> 0 is in {3/2, 2}.
entries.multi_part_probe: For the classical obstacle problem (zero obstacle on a domain in R^n): (a) state Almgren's frequency monotonicity; (b) describe how the limit value of N(0+) classifies free-boundary points; (c) what is the regularity of the free boundary at a regular point and at a singular point?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) N(r) is non-decreasing in r for r small (Almgren-type frequency formula adapted to the obstacle problem); the limit N(0+) is 3/2 (regular point) or 2 (singular point); (b) regular points (N(0+) = 3/2) are where the contact set has positive density and the free boundary is locally a graph; singular points (N(0+) = 2) are where the contact set has zero density or higher contact; (c) at regular points the free boundary is C^{1, alpha} (Caffarelli 1977 -> later sharpened); singular points form a (n-1)-rectifiable set whose stratification by frequency is finer (Figalli-Serra 2019 type results show C^{1,1} on the free boundary in dimensions <= 4, with finer dimension bounds on the singular set).
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is monotonicity formula; multi-part adds free-boundary point classification + regularity
entries.source: Almgren (frequency, original 1979 preprint); Caffarelli 1977 for free-boundary regularity; Figalli-Serra 2019 (Inventiones) for singular set

entries.single_part_probe: What upper bound does Shelah's PCF theorem give on 2^ℵ_ω assuming ℵ_ω is a strong limit?
entries.single_part_expected: If ℵ_ω is a strong limit then 2^ℵ_ω < ℵ_{ω₄}
entries.multi_part_probe: Concerning Shelah's PCF bound on 2^ℵ_ω: (a) what upper bound does the theorem give assuming ℵ_ω is a strong limit? (b) what is the central PCF object whose cardinality controls the bound? (c) is the exponent 4 in ℵ_{ω₄} known to be optimal?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) if ℵ_ω is a strong limit then 2^ℵ_ω < ℵ_{ω₄}; (b) the bound is controlled by |pcf(A)| where A is an interval of regular cardinals cofinal in ℵ_ω; Shelah proved |pcf(A)| < |A|⁺⁴, which yields the ω₄ bound; (c) it is open whether 4 is optimal; ℵ_{ω₁} cannot be the exact value, but no specific countable α < ω₄ has been ruled out, and 2^ℵ_ω = ℵ_{α+1} is known consistent (Magidor) for any countable α modulo large cardinals
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
entries.source: Shelah, Cardinal Arithmetic (Oxford 1994); Shelah 2000 'Cardinal Arithmetic for Skeptics' (Sh 400a); Holz–Steffens–Weitz, Introduction to Cardinal Arithmetic

entries.single_part_probe: What is the consistency strength of the failure of SCH at ℵ_ω?
entries.single_part_expected: Equiconsistent with a measurable cardinal κ of Mitchell order o(κ) = κ⁺⁺
entries.multi_part_probe: For the failure of SCH at ℵ_ω: (a) what is the exact consistency strength? (b) which forcing construction realizes the upper bound? (c) what core-model technique gives the matching lower bound?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) equiconsistent with a measurable cardinal κ of Mitchell order o(κ) = κ⁺⁺ [Gitik–Mitchell 1996]; (b) Magidor 1977 supercompact-based forcing realizes the failure of SCH at ℵ_ω, with subsequent reductions of the supercompact hypothesis (eventually to o(κ)=κ⁺⁺ via Gitik’s forcing); (c) Mitchell-style core model construction recovers a measurable inner model of o(κ)=κ⁺⁺ from any failure of SCH
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
entries.source: Magidor 1977 (Ann. Math. 106); Gitik 1989; Gitik–Mitchell 1996 (Ann. Pure Appl. Logic 82)

entries.single_part_probe: What is the inapproximability ratio for SET-COVER?
entries.single_part_expected: (1 - o(1)) ln n; matched by greedy
entries.multi_part_probe: For SET-COVER on a ground set of size n: (a) what is the polynomial-time approximation ratio of the greedy algorithm? (b) what is the matching hardness? (c) under what assumption?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) ln n + 1 (greedy, since H_n approximately ln n); (b) (1 - o(1)) ln n is NP-hard to approximate by Dinur-Steurer 2014 J. ACM; (c) only P != NP required (no stronger assumption)
entries.decomposition_target: tests separation of algorithmic ratio (b/a) from hardness assumption (c)
entries.source: Dinur-Steurer 2014 J. ACM (improving Feige 1998 under stronger assumption)

entries.single_part_probe: What is the deterministic communication complexity of EQUALITY on n-bit inputs?
entries.single_part_expected: Theta(n)
entries.multi_part_probe: Communication complexity of EQUALITY on n-bit strings: (a) deterministic, (b) randomized (public coin), (c) quantum?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Theta(n) deterministically (must read essentially all bits in adversarial alignment); (b) O(log n) with public-coin randomness via fingerprinting (e.g., reduction modulo a random prime); O(log n) with private-coin randomness as well by Newman's theorem; (c) Theta(log n) — quantum gives no asymptotic improvement over public-coin randomized for EQUALITY
entries.decomposition_target: tests separation across three CC models for the same function — a canonical multi-part question
entries.source: Yao 1979 STOC; Newman 1991; Buhrman-Cleve-Wigderson 1998

entries.single_part_probe: Define Wilf equivalence of permutation patterns.
entries.single_part_expected: Two patterns sigma, tau are Wilf-equivalent iff |Av_n(sigma)| = |Av_n(tau)| for all n.
entries.multi_part_probe: Wilf equivalence: (a) define it; (b) which length-4 patterns are Wilf-equivalent? (c) does Wilf equivalence respect symmetry-group action (reverse, complement, inverse)?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) |Av_n(sigma)| = |Av_n(tau)| for all n; (b) length-4 splits into 3 Wilf classes: {1234, 1243, 2143, 1432, 2134, 3214, 4321, 4312, 3412, 4231, 3421, 4132, 2341, 3124} - actually the 24 patterns split into 3 Wilf classes of sizes 7, 8, 9 once trivial symmetry is quotiented; the three nontrivial-class representatives are 1342 (class of size 7 mod trivial sym), 1234 (class of size 8), 1324 (class of size 9); (c) yes — the 8 trivial symmetries (D_4 action: reverse, complement, inverse, and compositions) always preserve Wilf class.
entries.decomposition_target: decomposition wrapper must surface the symmetry-group quotient correctly
entries.source: Stankova 1994; Backelin-West-Xin 2007 (J. Combin. Theory Ser. A) for the 1234-1432 Wilf equivalence chain

entries.single_part_probe: What is the value of the Feigenbaum constant delta in the period-doubling cascade for unimodal maps with quadratic critical point?
entries.multi_part_probe: For the period-doubling cascade in a one-parameter family of unimodal maps f_a with quadratic critical point: (a) what is the numerical value of Feigenbaum's constant delta? (b) what is the Feigenbaum constant alpha and the geometric quantity it measures? (c) describe the conceptual basis (renormalization fixed point of an operator on the space of unimodal maps) that explains universality of delta and alpha across families.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) delta ~ 4.66920160910299 = lim_n (a_n - a_{n-1})/(a_{n+1} - a_n), where a_n is the parameter at which a period-2^n orbit is born; (b) alpha ~ 2.50290787509589, the asymptotic ratio of widths of successive bifurcation branches at the critical point (alpha = lim_n d_n / d_{n+1}, with d_n the distance from the critical value to the nearest period-2^n point); (c) the doubling renormalization operator R(f)(x) = (1/alpha) f(f(alpha x)) on the space of unimodal maps has a hyperbolic fixed point f_*; the unstable manifold is one-dimensional with eigenvalue delta, and alpha is the scaling factor in the fixed-point equation; this explains universality across smooth families (Feigenbaum 1978, rigorously established by Lanford 1982 with computer-assisted proof).
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer when the model can answer single-part correctly
entries.source: Feigenbaum, J. Stat. Phys. 19 (1978); Lanford, Bull. AMS 6 (1982); de Melo and van Strien, 'One-Dimensional Dynamics' (1993)

entries.single_part_probe: State the Riesz-Thorin interpolation theorem.
entries.single_part_expected: If T is bounded L^{p_0} -> L^{q_0} with norm M_0 and L^{p_1} -> L^{q_1} with norm M_1, then T is bounded L^{p_theta} -> L^{q_theta} with norm at most M_0^{1-theta} M_1^theta, where (1/p_theta, 1/q_theta) = (1-theta)(1/p_0, 1/q_0) + theta(1/p_1, 1/q_1) for theta in [0,1].
entries.multi_part_probe: For Riesz-Thorin interpolation: (a) state the theorem; (b) what is the proof technique? (c) compare to Marcinkiewicz interpolation: which one allows weak-type endpoints, and what is the cost in the constant?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) standard Riesz-Thorin statement (see single_part_expected); (b) proof uses the three-lines lemma applied to the analytic family T_z = M_0^{1-z} M_1^z M_0^{-1+z} ... (Hadamard three-lines / Phragmen-Lindelof on the strip 0 <= Re z <= 1); (c) Marcinkiewicz allows weak-type endpoints (T: L^{p_i,infinity} -> L^{q_i,infinity}) but produces a worse constant that blows up as theta -> 0 or 1; Riesz-Thorin requires strong-type and gives the multiplicative-log-convex constant M_0^{1-theta} M_1^theta.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is theorem statement; multi-part folds in proof technique + comparison to Marcinkiewicz
entries.source: Riesz 1927; Thorin 1939; Marcinkiewicz 1939 (posthumous Stein-Weiss)

entries.single_part_probe: State the Bourgain-Demeter l^2-decoupling theorem for the truncated paraboloid.
entries.single_part_expected: For the truncated paraboloid in R^n and a delta-neighborhood of it tessellated into delta^{1/2}-caps, the L^p extension norm decouples into the l^2 sum of cap-extensions with sharp loss delta^{-epsilon} for p in the conjectural range p <= 2(n+1)/(n-1).
entries.multi_part_probe: For the Bourgain-Demeter l^2-decoupling theorem on the paraboloid: (a) state the decoupling inequality; (b) describe its sharp range; (c) name two classical results that follow as corollaries.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Bourgain-Demeter 2015 Annals: ||sum_theta f_theta||_{L^p(R^n)} <= C_epsilon delta^{-epsilon} (sum_theta ||f_theta||^2_{L^p})^{1/2} for p in [2, 2(n+1)/(n-1)] and f_theta supported in the theta-cap on the truncated paraboloid (delta-tessellation, theta caps of size delta^{1/2}); (b) the range is sharp at p = 2(n+1)/(n-1) (matches Stein-Tomas) up to delta^{-epsilon}-loss, and is open for finer constants; (c) implies (i) Vinogradov's main conjecture for diagonal systems, with applications to Waring-type problems (Bourgain-Demeter-Guth 2016 Annals); (ii) sharp Strichartz-type inequalities and dispersive PDE regularity bounds; also gives (iii) the latest progress on Lindelof-direction zeta-function exponents.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is theorem; multi-part adds range + corollaries (Vinogradov, dispersive PDE)
entries.source: Bourgain-Demeter 2015 (Annals of Mathematics, 'The proof of the l^2 decoupling conjecture'); Bourgain-Demeter-Guth 2016 (Annals)

entries.single_part_probe: What is the Sipser-Lautemann theorem?
entries.single_part_expected: BPP is contained in Sigma_2 ∩ Pi_2 (the second level of the polynomial hierarchy).
entries.multi_part_probe: About BPP and PH: (a) what is the Sipser-Lautemann result? (b) who proved it? (c) what stronger containment is known?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) BPP is contained in Sigma_2^P ∩ Pi_2^P; (b) Sipser 1983 STOC and Gács; Lautemann 1983 (independently with simpler proof); (c) BPP is contained in P/poly (Adleman 1978); but BPP is not unconditionally contained in P, and the question 'BPP = P?' is unsettled (closely tied to circuit lower bounds via Impagliazzo-Wigderson)
entries.decomposition_target: tests separation of statement (a), authorship (b), and stronger-containment context (c)
entries.source: Sipser 1983 STOC; Lautemann 1983; Adleman 1978 FOCS

entries.single_part_probe: What does the Burgisser-Ikenmeyer-Panova 2017 result say about GCT?
entries.single_part_expected: There are no occurrence obstructions in geometric complexity theory for the determinant-vs-permanent problem.
entries.multi_part_probe: About GCT obstructions: (a) what does BIP 2017 prove? (b) does this kill the GCT program? (c) what refinement of GCT remains open?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Burgisser-Ikenmeyer-Panova 2017 J. entries.single_part_probe: State the Martin–Steel theorem (1989) connecting Woodin cardinals and projective determinacy.
entries.single_part_expected: Martin–Steel (1989) proved: if there exist n Woodin cardinals with a measurable cardinal above them, then Π¹_{n+1}-determinacy holds; in particular, ω Woodin cardinals with a measurable above implies projective determinacy (PD)
entries.multi_part_probe: Concerning the Martin–Steel theorem: (a) state the implication from Woodin cardinals to projective determinacy; (b) what is the converse direction (Woodin's theorem)? (c) what regularity properties does PD imply for projective sets?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Martin–Steel (1989) proved: if there exist n Woodin cardinals with a measurable cardinal above them, then Π¹_{n+1}-determinacy holds; in particular, ω Woodin cardinals with a measurable above implies projective determinacy (PD); (b) Woodin (1988, refined later) proved a converse: PD implies the existence of inner models with n Woodin cardinals for every n; combined with Martin–Steel this gives that PD is equiconsistent with 'there are infinitely many Woodin cardinals'; (c) PD implies that every projective set of reals is Lebesgue measurable, has the Baire property, has the perfect-set property, is uniformizable (in a definable way at appropriate levels), and that the projective sets satisfy strong regularity uniformly across the projective hierarchy
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
entries.source: Martin–Steel, 'A proof of projective determinacy' J. Amer. Math. Soc. 2 (1989), 71–125; Woodin 1988 (PNAS 85); Neeman, The Determinacy of Long Games (2004)

entries.single_part_probe: What is the chromatic number of the plane (Hadwiger-Nelson problem)?
entries.single_part_expected: Currently bounded between 5 (de Grey 2018, Exoo-Ismailescu independent) and 7 (classical hexagonal-tiling upper bound).
entries.multi_part_probe: Hadwiger-Nelson chromatic number of the plane: (a) state the problem; (b) what was the historical bound and what changed in 2018? (c) what is the current best upper bound?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) chi(R^2) = minimum number of colors needed to color the plane so that no two points at distance exactly 1 share a color; (b) historically 4 <= chi(R^2) <= 7 from the 1950s (Nelson, Hadwiger), unchanged for 60+ years; in 2018 Aubrey de Grey constructed a 5-chromatic unit-distance graph (arxiv 1804.02385), pushing the lower bound to 5; subsequent Polymath 16 work refined the construction; (c) upper bound 7 from the regular hexagonal tiling with 7 colors (each hexagon colored, diameter < 1) remains best.
entries.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface de Grey 2018 lower-bound jump and refusal to claim above-7 upper bound
entries.source: de Grey 2018 (arxiv 1804.02385); classical 4 <= chi <= 7 unchanged 1950s-2017

entries.single_part_probe: State Furstenberg's multiple recurrence theorem.
entries.single_part_expected: For any measure-preserving T on a probability space (X, B, mu), any A in B with mu(A) > 0, and any k >= 1, there exists n >= 1 with mu(A cap T^{-n} A cap T^{-2n} A cap ... cap T^{-kn} A) > 0.
entries.multi_part_probe: For Furstenberg's ergodic-theoretic approach to Szemeredi's theorem: (a) state Furstenberg's multiple recurrence theorem; (b) state the Furstenberg correspondence principle relating subsets of N of positive upper density to invariant measures on shift spaces; (c) explain how (a) and (b) together yield Szemeredi's theorem on arithmetic progressions.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) For any measure-preserving (X, B, mu, T), any A with mu(A) > 0, and any k >= 1, there exists n >= 1 with mu(A cap T^{-n} A cap T^{-2n} A cap ... cap T^{-kn} A) > 0; (b) Furstenberg correspondence: given E subset N of positive upper Banach density d*(E) > 0, there exist a probability measure-preserving system (X, B, mu, T) and a set A in B with mu(A) = d*(E) such that for all finite F subset Z, d*(intersection_{n in F} (E - n)) >= mu(intersection_{n in F} T^{-n} A); (c) given E with d*(E) > 0, apply correspondence to get (X,T,mu,A); apply (a) to get n with mu(A cap T^{-n}A cap ... cap T^{-kn}A) > 0; correspondence then implies d*(E cap (E-n) cap ... cap (E-kn)) > 0, so in particular this set is nonempty, giving an arithmetic progression a, a+n, ..., a+kn in E. This is Szemeredi's theorem.
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer when the model can answer single-part correctly
entries.source: Furstenberg, J. Anal. Math. 31 (1977); Furstenberg, 'Recurrence in Ergodic Theory and Combinatorial Number Theory' (1981); Szemeredi, Acta Arith. 27 (1975)

entries.single_part_probe: State Furstenberg's theorem on closed subsets of [0,1] simultaneously invariant under T_2(x) = 2x mod 1 and T_3(x) = 3x mod 1.
entries.single_part_expected: Every closed subset of [0,1] simultaneously invariant under both T_2 and T_3 is either finite (consisting of rationals with denominator coprime to 6) or all of [0,1].
entries.multi_part_probe: For the Furstenberg x2 x3 problem: (a) state Furstenberg's topological theorem on closed sets jointly invariant under x2 and x3 mod 1; (b) state Furstenberg's measure-theoretic conjecture on jointly invariant Borel probability measures and the partial result of Rudolph (entropy hypothesis); (c) explain the role of positive entropy with respect to one of the maps in Rudolph's theorem.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Furstenberg (1967): a closed subset of [0,1] invariant under both T_2 and T_3 is either finite (a finite union of rational orbits) or equals [0,1]; (b) Furstenberg conjectured that any non-atomic Borel probability measure mu invariant and ergodic under both T_2 and T_3 must be Lebesgue; Rudolph (1990) proved this assuming h_mu(T_2) > 0 (positive entropy w.r.t. T_2, equivalently w.r.t. T_3); the unconditional conjecture remains open; (c) the entropy assumption rules out the trivial obstruction of zero-entropy measures (which can be exotic for a single rotation-like factor); given positive entropy, Rudolph used a Markov partition / coding argument plus the rigidity from commuting actions to force absolute continuity, hence Lebesgue.
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer when the model can answer single-part correctly
entries.source: Furstenberg, Math. Syst. Theory 1 (1967); Rudolph, Ergod. Th. Dyn. Sys. 10 (1990); Lindenstrauss, Ann. of Math. 163 (2006)

entries.single_part_probe: State the Bohnenblust-Hille / Khintchine inequality for symmetric m-linear forms (or the related Kahane-Khintchine inequality for L^p norms of randomized signs).
entries.single_part_expected: Kahane-Khintchine: for any sequence (a_n) in a Banach space and Rademacher epsilon_n: ||sum epsilon_n a_n||_{L^p(Omega; X)} ~ ||sum epsilon_n a_n||_{L^q(Omega; X)} for 1 <= p, q < infinity, with constants depending only on p, q (and the Banach space's type/cotype).
entries.multi_part_probe: For random series and Banach-space-valued integrals: (a) state Kahane-Khintchine; (b) define the type and cotype of a Banach space; (c) state which spaces have type 2 and which have cotype 2 (give a representative example each), and what this implies for vector-valued harmonic analysis.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Kahane-Khintchine: L^p-norms of sum epsilon_n a_n are equivalent for all 1 <= p < infinity (with constants depending on p, q, X); (b) X has type p (in [1, 2]) if E ||sum epsilon_n a_n||^p_X <= C^p sum ||a_n||^p_X; X has cotype q (in [2, infinity]) if E ||sum epsilon_n a_n||^q_X >= c^q sum ||a_n||^q_X; (c) Hilbert spaces have type 2 and cotype 2 (sharp); L^p has type min(p, 2) and cotype max(p, 2); type 2 implies UMD (with caveats) and is needed for vector-valued Mihlin/Hormander multiplier theorems and for L^p-vector-valued singular integrals to remain bounded.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is K-K; multi-part adds type/cotype + vector-valued harmonic analysis
entries.source: Khintchine 1923; Kahane 1985 'Some random series of functions' (Cambridge); Maurey-Pisier 1976 (Studia Mathematica)

entries.single_part_probe: State the De Giorgi-Nash-Moser theorem on Holder regularity of solutions to divergence-form elliptic equations with bounded measurable coefficients.
entries.single_part_expected: For a uniformly elliptic divergence-form operator L u = div(A(x) grad u) on a domain in R^n with A in L^infinity uniformly elliptic, every weak solution u in W^{1,2} is locally Holder continuous: u in C^{0, alpha}_{loc} for some alpha = alpha(n, ellipticity) in (0, 1).
entries.multi_part_probe: For divergence-form elliptic equations with bounded measurable coefficients on R^n: (a) state the De Giorgi-Nash-Moser theorem; (b) what is the role of the Moser iteration / Harnack inequality in the proof; (c) what fails for non-divergence-form equations L u = sum a_{ij}(x) partial_i partial_j u with bounded measurable a_{ij}, and what ellipticity refinement closes the gap?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) De Giorgi-Nash-Moser: weak solutions are locally Holder continuous, with alpha depending only on n and the ellipticity ratio; (b) Moser's iteration uses the De Giorgi level-set decay (each level set's measure decays geometrically) iteratively to get a sup-bound from an L^p-bound; combined with the Harnack inequality (positive solutions on a ball are comparable on a smaller ball), this yields oscillation decay and hence Holder continuity; (c) for non-divergence form with merely bounded measurable coefficients, regularity is OPEN in dimensions n >= 3 in general; the Krylov-Safonov 1979 theorem proves Harnack and C^{0, alpha} regularity for non-divergence form when the leading coefficients are bounded measurable AND uniformly elliptic, and is the analogue closing the non-divergence gap.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is regularity; multi-part adds Moser iteration + non-divergence gap
entries.source: De Giorgi 1957 (Memoirs Acad. Sci. Torino); Nash 1958 (Amer. J. Math.); Moser 1960 (CPAM); Krylov-Safonov 1979 (Math. USSR-Izv.)

entries.single_part_probe: What does Raghavendra 2008 say about CSP approximation under UGC?
entries.single_part_expected: Under UGC, the basic SDP relaxation gives the optimal polynomial-time approximation ratio for every constraint satisfaction problem.
entries.multi_part_probe: For CSPs in general: (a) what is Raghavendra's 2008 result? (b) does this give explicit constants per CSP? (c) what does it say about gap-CSPs without UGC?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Raghavendra 2008: under UGC, the basic SDP achieves the optimal poly-time approximation for every CSP; (b) no — the result establishes existence of matching hardness, but the exact constants are not generally computable in closed form; (c) without UGC the result does not transfer; specific CSPs have unconditional hardness via PCP machinery (e.g. MAX-3SAT 7/8 by Hastad)
entries.decomposition_target: tests separation of structural existence claim (a) from computational explicitness (b) and from conditional/unconditional dependency (c)
entries.source: Raghavendra 2008 STOC

entries.single_part_probe: What is the consequence of MIP* = RE for Connes' embedding conjecture?
entries.single_part_expected: MIP* = RE implies Connes' embedding conjecture is false.
entries.multi_part_probe: About MIP* = RE: (a) what classes are equated? (b) who proved it and when? (c) what is the implication for operator algebras?

entries.single_part_probe: State the McKean-Vlasov SDE that arises as the mean-field limit of an N-particle system with pairwise interaction kernel b(x,y) and noise dW_t.
entries.single_part_expected: dX_t = (integral b(X_t, y) mu_t(dy)) dt + sigma dW_t, where mu_t = Law(X_t).
entries.multi_part_probe: For mean-field limits of weakly interacting diffusions dX_i^N = (1/N) sum_{j=1}^N b(X_i^N, X_j^N) dt + sigma dW_i, i=1,...,N: (a) state the limiting McKean-Vlasov SDE; (b) state Sznitman's propagation-of-chaos result on the convergence of the joint law of any k particles; (c) describe the Fokker-Planck (nonlinear) PDE satisfied by the law mu_t.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) dX_t = (integral b(X_t, y) mu_t(dy)) dt + sigma dW_t with mu_t = Law(X_t); (b) Sznitman: under Lipschitz b, for any fixed k, the law of (X_1^N,...,X_k^N) converges weakly to mu_t^{otimes k} as N -> infinity, so any k tagged particles become asymptotically independent and identically distributed (propagation of chaos); (c) the density rho_t of mu_t satisfies the nonlinear Fokker-Planck / McKean-Vlasov PDE: partial_t rho = (sigma^2 / 2) Delta rho - div(rho (b * rho)), where (b * rho)(x) := integral b(x,y) rho(y) dy.
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer when the model can answer single-part correctly
entries.source: McKean, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 56 (1966); Sznitman, Ecole d'Ete de Saint-Flour XIX (1991), Springer LNM 1464

entries.single_part_probe: What is Gödel's constructible universe L, and what does it satisfy?
entries.single_part_expected: L is the class defined by L_0 = ∅, L_{α+1} = Def(L_α), L_λ = ⋃_{α<λ} L_α; L is an inner model of ZFC and satisfies V = L, GCH, and ⋄ (Jensen's diamond at every uncountable regular)
entries.multi_part_probe: Concerning Gödel's constructible universe L: (a) define L and state the principal axioms it satisfies; (b) which large cardinals are absolute to L, and which become inconsistent in L? (c) what is Jensen's covering lemma, and what does it say about V vs. L when 0⁻ does not exist?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) L is the class defined by L_0 = ∅, L_{α+1} = Def(L_α), L_λ = ⋃_{α<λ} L_α; L is an inner model of ZFC and satisfies V = L, GCH, and ⋄ (Jensen's diamond at every uncountable regular cardinal); (b) inaccessible, Mahlo, weakly compact, ineffable, and (more generally) all small large cardinals up to 0⁻ are absolute to L; from a measurable cardinal upward, large cardinals are inconsistent with V = L — Scott showed measurable cardinals contradict V = L; (c) Jensen's covering lemma states that if 0⁻ does not exist, then every uncountable set of ordinals is contained in a constructible set of the same cardinality; equivalently, V is 'close' to L in the sense that singular cardinals of V are singular in L
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
entries.source: Gödel 1940; Scott 1961 (JSL 26); Jensen 1972 (Ann. Math. Logic 4); Devlin–Jensen, 'Marginalia to a theorem of Silver'

entries.single_part_probe: State Freiman's theorem on sets with small doubling.
entries.single_part_expected: If A subset Z is finite with |A+A| <= K|A|, then A is contained in a generalized arithmetic progression of dimension d <= d(K) and size <= f(K) * |A|, where d(K) and f(K) depend only on K.
entries.multi_part_probe: Freiman's theorem: (a) state the structure theorem; (b) what is the best known dependence f(K), d(K)? (c) what is its analog in arbitrary abelian groups?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Freiman 1973: |A+A| <= K|A| forces A inside a d-dimensional GAP of size <= f(K)|A|; (b) Sanders 2012 quasi-polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa gave dimension d = O((log K)^{3+o(1)}) and size f(K) = exp((log K)^{3+o(1)}) |A|; the polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa conjecture (PFR) asks whether d = O(log K) and f(K) = exp(O(log K)) = poly(K); PFR over F_2^n was proven by Gowers-Green-Manners-Tao 2023 (arxiv 2311.05762); (c) the F_2^n version (subgroup analogue) is the Polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa Conjecture; over arbitrary abelian groups Freiman has been generalized via Green-Ruzsa (2007).
entries.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface PFR-over-F_2 settled status (Gowers-Green-Manners-Tao 2023)
entries.source: Freiman 1973; Sanders 2012 (Anal. PDE); Gowers-Green-Manners-Tao 2023 (arxiv 2311.05762)

entries.single_part_probe: What is the Suslin Hypothesis, and what is the independence result for it?
entries.single_part_expected: Suslin Hypothesis (SH): every ccc dense linear order without endpoints that is order-complete is order-isomorphic to (ℝ, <); Tennenbaum (1968) and Jech (1967) constructed Suslin trees from ⋄ (so SH fails in L); Solovay–Tennenbaum (1971) used iterated ccc forcing to obtain Con(ZFC + SH); thus SH is independent of ZFC
entries.multi_part_probe: Concerning the Suslin Hypothesis: (a) state SH and its independence; (b) what is a Suslin tree, and how does its existence relate to SH? (c) what role did Suslin's problem play in motivating the development of forcing iterations and Martin's Axiom?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Suslin Hypothesis (SH): every ccc dense linear order without endpoints that is order-complete is order-isomorphic to (ℝ, <); Tennenbaum (1968) and Jech (1967) constructed Suslin trees from ⋄ (so SH fails in L); Solovay–Tennenbaum (1971) used iterated ccc forcing to obtain Con(ZFC + SH); thus SH is independent of ZFC; (b) a Suslin tree is an ℵ_1-tree with no uncountable chain or antichain; SH is equivalent to 'there are no Suslin trees', because the order-completion of a Suslin tree (suitably ordered) gives a Suslin line, and conversely; (c) solving Suslin's problem in the consistency direction required iterating ccc forcings to kill all potential Suslin trees, which led directly to Martin's Axiom and the systematic theory of finite-support ccc iterations — Solovay–Tennenbaum 1971 is the foundational paper
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
entries.source: Suslin 1920 (Fund. Math. 1); Tennenbaum 1968 (PNAS 59); Jech 1967 (Czech. Math. J. 17); Solovay–Tennenbaum 1971 (Ann. Math. 94)

entries.single_part_probe: What is the Wolff lower bound on the Hausdorff dimension of Kakeya sets in R^n?
entries.single_part_expected: dim_H >= (n+2)/2 for all n >= 2 (Wolff 1995, hairbrush argument).
entries.multi_part_probe: For Kakeya sets in R^n: (a) state the Wolff hairbrush lower bound; (b) what is the conjectured dimension; (c) what is the current best lower bound in n=3 specifically and who proved the most recent improvement?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) dim_H >= (n+2)/2 (Wolff 1995); (b) conjectured dim_H = n; (c) in n=3 the Wolff bound 5/2 was improved to 5/2 + epsilon for small explicit epsilon by Katz-Tao (early 2000s) and again most recently by Wang-Zahl 2022 (arxiv 2207.01054), who push past the Wolff threshold via decoupling-style techniques.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is Wolff bound; multi-part adds conjecture + recent progress
entries.source: Wolff 1995; Katz-Tao early 2000s; Wang-Zahl arxiv 2207.01054 (2022)

entries.single_part_probe: Define a factor map between measure-preserving systems (X, B, mu, T) and (Y, C, nu, S).
entries.single_part_expected: A factor map is a measurable map pi: X -> Y (defined mu-a.e.) such that pi_* mu = nu and pi composed with T equals S composed with pi mu-a.e.; equivalently a T-invariant sub-sigma-algebra C' = pi^{-1}(C) of B realizes (Y, C, nu, S) as a factor of (X, B, mu, T).
entries.multi_part_probe: For factors and the Pinsker / Kronecker structure of measure-preserving systems: (a) define a factor map between two measure-preserving systems; (b) define the Kronecker (maximal equicontinuous) factor and the Pinsker (maximal zero-entropy) factor; (c) state the Furstenberg-Zimmer structure theorem on the tower of distal extensions building toward the full system from the Kronecker factor.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) A factor map pi: (X, B, mu, T) -> (Y, C, nu, S) is a measurable map (mod null sets) with pi_* mu = nu and pi T = S pi mu-a.e.; equivalently it is given by a T-invariant sub-sigma-algebra of B; (b) the Kronecker factor is the largest factor on which T acts as a translation on a compact abelian group (equivalently, the factor generated by the eigenfunctions of the Koopman operator); the Pinsker factor is the largest factor of zero entropy and is sigma-algebra-generated by all zero-entropy sub-sigma-algebras (equivalently the orthogonal complement of the K-part); (c) Furstenberg-Zimmer (1977): every ergodic measure-preserving system has a canonical (transfinite) tower of factors X -> ... -> X_alpha -> ... -> X_1 -> Kronecker factor -> point, where each X_{alpha+1} -> X_alpha is a compact (isometric) extension and limits are taken at limit ordinals; the inverse limit is the maximal distal factor of X. The complement of distal in X is the weakly mixing part, so every ergodic system decomposes relative to this tower into distal-then-weakly-mixing structure.
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer when the model can answer single-part correctly
entries.source: Furstenberg, J. Anal. Math. 31 (1977); Zimmer, Illinois J. Math. 20 (1976); Furstenberg, 'Recurrence in Ergodic Theory and Combinatorial Number Theory' (1981) Ch. 5-6

entries.single_part_probe: What does BQP stand for and what is its standard definition?
entries.single_part_expected: Bounded-error Quantum Polynomial time: languages decided by a polynomial-time uniform family of quantum circuits with error bounded by 1/3 on every input.
entries.multi_part_probe: About BQP: (a) what is the formal definition? (b) what is the best known classical-vs-quantum oracle separation? (c) what is BQP's relationship to PH unconditionally?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Languages L for which there exists a uniform poly-time quantum circuit family that accepts x in L with probability >= 2/3 and accepts x not in L with probability <= 1/3; (b) Aaronson 2010 gave an oracle relative to which BQP is not contained in PH (specifically not in P^PP); Raz-Tal 2019 gave an oracle separating BQP from PH; (c) BQP vs PH is unconditionally open — neither containment is known without an oracle
entries.decomposition_target: tests separation of definition (a) from relativized result (b) from unconditional status (c)
entries.source: Bernstein-Vazirani 1997 SICOMP; Aaronson 2010; Raz-Tal 2019 STOC

entries.single_part_expected: 7/8 - epsilon for any epsilon > 0; matched by the random-assignment ratio 7/8

entries.single_part_probe: What is the Stanley-Wilf limit for a pattern of length k (existence statement)?
entries.single_part_expected: For every permutation pattern sigma of length k, the limit L(sigma) = lim_{n -> infty} |Av_n(sigma)|^{1/n} exists and is finite.
entries.multi_part_probe: Stanley-Wilf conjecture: (a) state the existence claim; (b) name the proof and approach; (c) what is L(sigma) for sigma = 1342 explicitly?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) the limit |Av_n(sigma)|^{1/n} exists and is bounded as n -> infinity, i.e. |Av_n(sigma)| <= c^n for some c = c(sigma); (b) proven by Marcus and Tardos (2004) via excluded-permutation-matrices reformulation, using Klazar's reduction (2000) of Stanley-Wilf to Füredi-Hajnal; (c) L(1342) = 8 (Bona 1997, exact value).

entries.single_part_probe: What does it mean for a basic set Lambda of an Axiom A diffeomorphism to admit a Markov partition?
entries.single_part_expected: There is a finite cover of Lambda by 'rectangles' R_i = {y in Lambda : y in W^s_loc(x_i) cap W^u_loc(x_j)-style products} with disjoint interiors and the property that f maps unstable boundaries of rectangles into unstable boundaries and stable boundaries into stable boundaries; equivalently the symbolic coding from R_i is conjugate to a SFT on the alphabet of rectangles.
entries.multi_part_probe: For Axiom A basic sets and their symbolic coding: (a) define a Markov partition of a basic set Lambda; (b) state Bowen's theorem on existence of Markov partitions for basic sets and the resulting semiconjugacy with a SFT; (c) describe how this is used to count periodic points and to construct equilibrium states (Gibbs measures) on Lambda.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) A Markov partition of Lambda is a finite cover {R_1,...,R_N} of Lambda by closed rectangles (each R_i has local product structure: R_i = {[x,y] : x in R_i^s, y in R_i^u} with [.,.] the Smale bracket) such that the rectangles have pairwise-disjoint interiors and satisfy the Markov property: if x in int(R_i) and f(x) in int(R_j), then f(W^u_loc(x) cap R_i) contains W^u_loc(f(x)) cap R_j and f(W^s_loc(x) cap R_i) is contained in W^s_loc(f(x)) cap R_j; (b) Bowen (1970,1975): every Axiom A basic set admits Markov partitions of arbitrarily small diameter, giving a Holder-continuous finite-to-one semiconjugacy pi: X_M -> Lambda from a SFT X_M with transition matrix M_{ij} = 1{f(int R_i) cap int R_j != empty}; (c) periodic points: |Per_n(f|Lambda)| = trace(M^n) up to boundary corrections; equilibrium states: for any Holder phi on Lambda, there is a unique invariant measure mu_phi maximizing h_mu(f) + integral phi dmu, constructed by lifting via pi to a Gibbs state on the SFT, where Bowen's formalism gives explicit transfer-operator construction.
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer when the model can answer single-part correctly

entries.single_part_probe: Define an Anosov diffeomorphism on a compact Riemannian manifold M.
entries.single_part_expected: A C^1 diffeomorphism f: M -> M is Anosov if there is a continuous Df-invariant splitting TM = E^s + E^u and constants C > 0, lambda in (0,1) such that ||Df^n v|| <= C lambda^n ||v|| for v in E^s and ||Df^{-n} v|| <= C lambda^n ||v|| for v in E^u, for all n >= 0.
entries.multi_part_probe: For Anosov diffeomorphisms: (a) state the definition (uniform hyperbolicity on all of M); (b) state Anosov's structural stability theorem; (c) state the spectral decomposition / classification: every known transitive Anosov diffeomorphism on a compact manifold is topologically conjugate to a hyperbolic toral automorphism on an infranilmanifold (Franks-Manning theorem for tori).
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Continuous Df-invariant splitting TM = E^s + E^u with uniform exponential contraction on E^s and expansion on E^u: ||Df^n|_{E^s}|| <= C lambda^n, ||Df^{-n}|_{E^u}|| <= C lambda^n, n >= 0; (b) Anosov's structural stability theorem (1967): every Anosov diffeomorphism is C^1-structurally stable, i.e., every f' sufficiently C^1-close is topologically conjugate to f via a homeomorphism close to identity; (c) Franks (1969) and Manning (1974): every Anosov diffeomorphism on the n-torus T^n is topologically conjugate to a hyperbolic toral automorphism induced by an integer matrix in GL(n, Z) with no eigenvalue on the unit circle; more generally, every transitive Anosov diffeomorphism on a compact infranilmanifold is conjugate to an algebraic hyperbolic automorphism, and it is open whether all manifolds supporting Anosov diffeomorphisms are infranilmanifolds.

entries.single_part_probe: State the Birman-Schwinger principle for counting bound states of a Schrodinger operator -Delta + V on R^n.
entries.single_part_expected: The number of negative eigenvalues of H = -Delta + V on R^n (with V suitably decaying) equals the number of eigenvalues >= 1 of the Birman-Schwinger operator K_E := |V|^{1/2} (-Delta - E)^{-1} V^{1/2} for E in (-infinity, 0) approaching 0; equivalently, N(V) = #{ev of K_0 >= 1}.
entries.multi_part_probe: For the Schrodinger operator H = -Delta + V on R^n with V <= 0, V in L^{n/2} (n >= 3): (a) state the Birman-Schwinger principle; (b) what does the principle give as an upper bound on the number of negative eigenvalues N(V); (c) state the Cwikel-Lieb-Rozenblum (CLR) inequality and how it relates.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Birman-Schwinger: N(V) = number of eigenvalues >= 1 of K_0; (b) the trace inequality N(V) <= Tr(K_0^q) for q chosen to make the trace finite; for n >= 3 one can take q = n/2 giving N(V) <= C_n integral |V|^{n/2}; (c) Cwikel-Lieb-Rozenblum (CLR): N(V) <= C_n integral |V_-|^{n/2} for n >= 3, where V_- = max(-V, 0) is the negative part; this is the sharp scaling of the bound, with explicit constants worked out by Cwikel 1977, Lieb 1976, Rozenblum 1972 independently; the CLR inequality is the canonical L^{n/2}-bound on bound-state counts.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is principle; multi-part adds the L^{n/2} bound + CLR
entries.source: Birman 1961; Schwinger 1961; Cwikel 1977; Lieb 1976; Rozenblum 1972

entries.single_part_probe: State the Carleson-Hunt theorem on a.e. convergence of Fourier series.
entries.single_part_expected: For f in L^p([0, 2pi]) with 1 < p <= infinity, the partial sums of the Fourier series of f converge to f almost everywhere (Carleson 1966 for p=2; Hunt 1968 for 1 < p < infinity).
entries.multi_part_probe: For Fourier series of L^p functions on the torus: (a) state Carleson-Hunt; (b) what is the role of the Carleson maximal operator C f(x) = sup_N |S_N f(x)|; (c) what fails at p=1 and how does the L log L threshold appear?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) a.e. convergence of partial sums S_N f -> f for f in L^p, 1 < p <= infinity (Carleson 1966, Hunt 1968); (b) the Carleson maximal operator C is bounded on L^p for 1 < p < infinity; this implies a.e. convergence via the standard maximal-operator-controls-pointwise-convergence argument; (c) at p=1 the Carleson operator is NOT bounded on L^1; Kolmogorov constructed an L^1 function whose Fourier series diverges everywhere. The threshold for a.e. convergence is L log L (Antonov 1996): if f is in L log L log log L the series converges a.e., and counterexamples in L log log log L are known (sharp class is bounded between these).

entries.source: Carleson 1966 (Acta Mathematica); Hunt 1968 (proceedings); Antonov 1996; Kolmogorov 1923

entries.single_part_probe: Define NIP (the non-independence property) for a complete first-order theory.
entries.single_part_expected: A complete theory T is NIP iff no formula φ(x; y) has the independence property in T — equivalently, for every φ(x; y) there is no model M of T and tuples (a_i)_{i<ω} in M such that for every S ⊆ ω there exists b in M with φ(a_i; b) ↔ i ∈ S

entries.single_part_probe: State the formula for the topological entropy of a topologically mixing subshift of finite type with transition matrix M.
entries.single_part_expected: h_top = log lambda_M, where lambda_M is the spectral radius (Perron eigenvalue) of M.
entries.multi_part_probe: For a topologically mixing subshift of finite type X_M with 0-1 transition matrix M on alphabet of size n: (a) state the entropy formula in terms of M; (b) describe the Parry measure as the unique measure of maximal entropy and its transition probabilities; (c) explain the role of the Perron-Frobenius theorem in justifying both (a) and (b).
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) h_top(X_M) = log lambda_M with lambda_M the largest eigenvalue (spectral radius) of M; (b) the Parry measure is the Markov measure with transition probabilities p_{ij} = M_{ij} v_j / (lambda_M v_i) and stationary distribution pi_i = u_i v_i, where u (left) and v (right) are positive eigenvectors of M for lambda_M normalized by sum u_i v_i = 1; this Markov chain is the unique shift-invariant measure attaining h_top = log lambda_M; (c) Perron-Frobenius for irreducible aperiodic non-negative matrices: lambda_M is real, simple, dominates all other eigenvalues in modulus, and has strictly positive left/right eigenvectors u, v; this guarantees existence of the Parry measure and that the count of admissible n-blocks grows like lambda_M^n, giving h_top = log lambda_M.
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer when the model can answer single-part correctly

entries.single_part_probe: What is the Escauriaza-Seregin-Sverak L^3-critical regularity criterion for 3D Navier-Stokes?
entries.single_part_expected: If a Leray-Hopf weak solution u of 3D NS satisfies u in L^infinity_t L^3_x on [0, T], then u is smooth past T; the L^3_x norm is the critical norm under NS scaling.
entries.multi_part_probe: For 3D incompressible Navier-Stokes: (a) state the Escauriaza-Seregin-Sverak L^3-critical criterion; (b) what is the scaling argument that identifies L^3 as critical; (c) what is the conceptual gap between L^3-critical and the supercritical L^2-energy norm (and why is this 'the open question')?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) ESS: u in L^infinity_t L^3_x (continuously) implies smoothness; (b) NS scaling u(x,t) -> lambda u(lambda x, lambda^2 t); a norm is critical if invariant under this rescaling; ||u||_{L^3_x} scales as lambda^0 (critical); ||u||_{L^2_x} scales as lambda^{-1/2} (supercritical, controlled by energy but does not bound the equation under rescaling); (c) global regularity is open because the only quantity bounded a priori by initial data is energy (supercritical); ESS gives smoothness if the L^3 norm is bounded but no a priori L^3 bound is known. Closing this gap is the heart of the Millennium NS problem.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is ESS criterion; multi-part adds scaling + the supercritical-vs-critical gap

entries.single_part_probe: Define a sofic shift, and state how it differs from a subshift of finite type.
entries.single_part_expected: A sofic shift is a factor (continuous shift-commuting image) of a subshift of finite type; equivalently, the set of bi-infinite paths in a labeled finite directed graph (where labels can repeat). Every SFT is sofic, but not conversely: sofic shifts can have memory of unbounded length (the even shift {sequences in {0,1}^Z with an even number of 0s between consecutive 1s} is sofic but not SFT).
entries.multi_part_probe: For sofic shifts: (a) define a sofic shift and contrast with subshifts of finite type; (b) give the canonical example of a sofic-but-not-SFT shift (the even shift) and explain why it fails the SFT property; (c) state how the topological entropy of a sofic shift is computed via the Perron eigenvalue of any presentation graph and why this is independent of the choice of presentation.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) A sofic shift is the image of an SFT under a continuous shift-commuting map (a 1-block or higher-block factor map); equivalently, the set of label sequences read off bi-infinite walks on a finite labeled directed graph; SFTs are sofic via the identity factor map, but sofic shifts include factors that lose the finite-memory property; (b) the even shift is the set of bi-infinite {0,1} sequences in which between any two consecutive 1s there are an even number of 0s; it is presented by a 2-vertex graph with labels 0 and 1, hence sofic, but is not SFT because forbidden words have unbounded length (1 0^{2k+1} 1 is forbidden for every k); (c) for any irreducible labeled-graph presentation G with underlying transition matrix M_G, h_top = log lambda(M_G) where lambda is the Perron eigenvalue; different presentations of the same sofic shift give the same h_top because they are factor-equivalent and topological entropy is a conjugacy/factor invariant (factor maps don't increase entropy and a minimal-state presentation realizes the entropy).

entries.source: Weiss, Monatsh. Math. 77 (1973); Lind and Marcus, 'An Introduction to Symbolic Dynamics and Coding' (1995) Ch. 3
