entries.single_part_probe: State Ramsey's theorem (finite version) and the key existence claim.
entries.single_part_expected: For all r, k_1, ..., k_r positive integers, there exists N = R(k_1, ..., k_r; r) such that any r-coloring of edges of K_N contains a monochromatic K_{k_i} in color i for some i.
entries.multi_part_probe: Ramsey's theorem (1930): (a) state the finite version with r colors; (b) state the infinite version; (c) what about hypergraph Ramsey?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) for r colors and target sizes k_1, ..., k_r, there exists finite N(r; k_1, ..., k_r) such that any r-edge-coloring of K_N has a color-i monochromatic K_{k_i} for some i; (b) infinite version: any r-coloring of edges of an infinite complete graph contains an infinite monochromatic complete subgraph; (c) hypergraph Ramsey R^{(t)}(k; r) on t-uniform hypergraphs: bounds grow tower-of-exponentials in k for t >= 3 (Erdős-Hajnal-Rado), and the lower bounds match the upper bounds at the tower level for t >= 4 (Erdős-Rado).
entries.decomposition_target: wrapper must distinguish graph (t=2) from hypergraph (t>=3) Ramsey complexity classes
entries.source: Ramsey 1930; Erdős-Hajnal-Rado 1965 for hypergraph version

entries.single_part_probe: What is the Pesin entropy formula for a C^{1+alpha} diffeomorphism f preserving an absolutely continuous probability measure mu (e.g., Lebesgue)?
entries.single_part_expected: h_mu(f) = integral sum_i max(lambda_i(x), 0) dim E_i(x) dmu(x), the integral of the sum of positive Lyapunov exponents counted with multiplicity.
entries.multi_part_probe: For a C^{1+alpha} diffeomorphism f of a compact manifold preserving a probability measure mu: (a) state the Ruelle inequality between h_mu(f) and the Lyapunov spectrum; (b) state the Pesin entropy formula and its hypothesis on mu; (c) explain how this characterizes SRB measures via 'entropy equals sum of positive Lyapunov exponents'.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Ruelle inequality (1978): h_mu(f) <= integral sum_i max(lambda_i(x), 0) dim E_i(x) dmu(x), valid for any C^1 diffeomorphism and any invariant Borel probability mu; (b) Pesin (1977): if mu is absolutely continuous w.r.t. Lebesgue (or more generally has absolutely continuous conditionals on unstable manifolds) and f is C^{1+alpha}, then equality holds: h_mu(f) = integral sum_i lambda_i^+ dim E_i dmu; (c) Ledrappier-Young (1985) showed an invariant measure mu satisfying the Pesin entropy formula is exactly an SRB measure (absolutely continuous conditionals on unstable manifolds), so the equality 'h = sum positive Lyapunov exponents' is the defining characterization of SRB.
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer when the model can answer single-part correctly
entries.source: Pesin, Russ. Math. Surveys 32 (1977); Ruelle, Bol. Soc. Brasil. Mat. 9 (1978); Ledrappier and Young, Ann. of Math. 122 (1985)

entries.single_part_probe: What does the Impagliazzo-Wigderson 1997 hardness-versus-randomness theorem say?
entries.single_part_expected: If E (linear-exponential time) requires circuits of size 2^Omega(n), then BPP = P.
entries.multi_part_probe: About Impagliazzo-Wigderson 1997: (a) what is the precise hypothesis? (b) what is the conclusion? (c) is this conditional or unconditional derandomization?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Impagliazzo-Wigderson 1997 STOC: if there is a language in DTIME(2^O(n)) that requires Boolean circuits of size 2^{Omega(n)} for all but finitely many input lengths; (b) then BPP = P; equivalently P^{BPP} = P; (c) conditional — the conclusion BPP = P depends on the existence of a hard function in E; unconditional BPP = P remains open as of standard knowledge
entries.decomposition_target: tests separation of hypothesis (a), conclusion (b), and the conditional-vs-unconditional caveat (c)
entries.source: Impagliazzo-Wigderson 1997 STOC

entries.single_part_probe: State the immediate predecessor of 3 in Sharkovsky's ordering of the natural numbers.
entries.single_part_expected: 5 (Sharkovsky's order begins 3 < 5 < 7 < 9 < ... so 5 is the next after 3 in the universal-forcing order).
entries.multi_part_probe: For Sharkovsky's theorem on continuous self-maps of an interval: (a) state Sharkovsky's ordering of N and the immediate successor of 3; (b) state Sharkovsky's theorem precisely (existence of period n implies existence of every period m with n preceding m in the ordering); (c) explain why period 3 implies all periods (the Li-Yorke 'period three implies chaos' corollary).
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) The Sharkovsky order is 3 < 5 < 7 < 9 < ... < 2*3 < 2*5 < ... < 2^2*3 < 2^2*5 < ... < ... < 2^n < ... < 8 < 4 < 2 < 1; the immediate successor of 3 is 5; (b) for any continuous f: I -> I, if f has a periodic point of period n and m succeeds n in the Sharkovsky order, then f also has a periodic point of period m; (c) since 3 is the maximum element, period-3 forces every period in N, which (with an additional argument) yields the Li-Yorke uncountable scrambled set ('period three implies chaos').
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer when the model can answer single-part correctly
entries.source: Sharkovsky, Ukrain. Mat. Zh. 16 (1964); Li and Yorke, Amer. Math. Monthly 82 (1975)

entries.single_part_probe: What is meant by an ergodic measure-preserving transformation T?
entries.single_part_expected: T-invariant measurable sets have measure 0 or 1; equivalently, every T-invariant L^2 function is constant a.e., or every T-invariant measurable function is constant a.e.
entries.multi_part_probe: For measure-preserving transformations T on (X, B, mu): (a) define ergodicity; (b) define the strictly stronger property of mixing (sometimes called strong mixing); (c) place these on the spectrum: ergodic, weak mixing, mixing, K-system, Bernoulli, with one inclusion-strict-implication in each direction.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) T is ergodic iff every T-invariant set has measure 0 or 1 (equivalently, T-invariant L^2 functions are constants a.e.); (b) T is mixing iff for all A, B in B, mu(A cap T^{-n} B) -> mu(A) mu(B) as n -> infinity; (c) the strict-inclusion chain is: Bernoulli => K-system => mixing => weak mixing => ergodic; weak mixing means (1/n) sum_{k<n} |mu(A cap T^{-k}B) - mu(A) mu(B)| -> 0; an irrational rotation is ergodic but not weak mixing; the [T, T^{-1}] transformation is K but not Bernoulli (Kalikow 1982); the Chacon transformation is weak mixing but not mixing; a Bernoulli shift is the strongest, defined as conjugate to ((Z^n)^Z, Bernoulli measure, shift).
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer when the model can answer single-part correctly
entries.source: Walters, 'An Introduction to Ergodic Theory' (1982) Ch. 1; Kalikow, Ann. of Math. 115 (1982)

entries.single_part_probe: What are the necessary divisibility conditions for the existence of a Steiner triple system on n points?
entries.single_part_expected: n ≡ 1 or 3 (mod 6).
entries.multi_part_probe: Steiner triple systems STS(n): (a) state the necessary divisibility conditions; (b) are they sufficient? (c) what is the count of non-isomorphic STS(13)?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) n ≡ 1 or 3 (mod 6) (equivalently: 6 divides n(n-1)/3 and 3 divides n-1, etc.); (b) yes — Kirkman 1847 proved sufficiency (every n satisfying the condition admits an STS); (c) STS(13) has exactly 2 non-isomorphic systems (the projective plane PG(2,3)-derived system and one other).
entries.decomposition_target: wrapper must produce small-n classification count
entries.source: Kirkman 1847; STS(13) classification is classical, see Colbourn-Dinitz Handbook of Combinatorial Designs

entries.single_part_probe: What is Razborov's 1985 result on monotone circuit complexity of CLIQUE?
entries.single_part_expected: CLIQUE requires super-polynomial-size monotone circuits.
entries.multi_part_probe: About Razborov 1985 monotone CLIQUE: (a) what is the lower bound? (b) what technique was used? (c) does the result lift to non-monotone circuits?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Razborov 1985: CLIQUE requires monotone circuits of size at least n^Omega(sqrt(k)) where k is the clique parameter; later improved to exponential by Alon-Boppana 1987; (b) approximation method (Razborov) and method of approximations refined; (c) no — Tardos 1988 showed there exist polynomial-time computable functions (like a perfect-matching-related variant) requiring super-polynomial monotone circuits, so monotone-circuit lower bounds do not transfer to non-monotone P/poly
entries.decomposition_target: tests bound (a), method (b), and the critical caveat that monotone bounds do not lift (c) — common confusion
entries.source: Razborov 1985 Math. Notes; Alon-Boppana 1987 Combinatorica; Tardos 1988 Combinatorica

entries.single_part_probe: What is the topological entropy of the beta-shift for beta > 1?
entries.single_part_expected: h_top = log beta.
entries.multi_part_probe: For Renyi's beta-expansion / beta-shift X_beta with beta > 1: (a) what is the topological entropy of X_beta? (b) describe the unique measure of maximal entropy (Parry's measure for the beta-shift) and its density relative to Lebesgue on [0,1]; (c) state Parry's characterization of which sequences in {0,1,...,floor(beta)}^N are admissible (lexicographic condition with respect to the beta-expansion of 1).
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) h_top(X_beta) = log beta (Renyi 1957, Parry 1960); (b) the Parry measure on [0,1] for the beta-transformation T_beta(x) = beta x mod 1 has density h_beta(x) = (1/F(beta)) sum_{n: T_beta^n(1) > x} beta^{-n} with respect to Lebesgue, where F(beta) is the normalizing constant making h_beta into a probability density; this measure is the unique measure of maximal entropy and equals the unique absolutely continuous invariant probability for T_beta; (c) Parry's admissibility theorem: a one-sided sequence (a_n) in {0,...,floor(beta)}^N is the beta-expansion of some x in [0,1) iff for every k >= 0, the shifted sequence (a_{k+1}, a_{k+2}, ...) is lexicographically strictly less than the beta-expansion d*(beta) of 1 (the so-called quasi-greedy expansion of 1), giving a sofic shift exactly when d*(beta) is eventually periodic.
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer when the model can answer single-part correctly
entries.source: Renyi, Acta Math. Acad. Sci. Hungar. 8 (1957); Parry, Acta Math. Acad. Sci. Hungar. 11 (1960)

entries.single_part_probe: State the Caffarelli-Kohn-Nirenberg partial regularity theorem for suitable weak solutions of 3D Navier-Stokes.
entries.single_part_expected: The singular set S in space-time of a suitable weak solution of 3D NS satisfies P^1(S) = 0, where P^1 is one-dimensional parabolic Hausdorff measure.
entries.multi_part_probe: For the 3D incompressible Navier-Stokes equations: (a) state the Caffarelli-Kohn-Nirenberg partial regularity theorem; (b) what is the definition of suitable weak solution that the theorem requires; (c) compare CKN's partial regularity to global regularity (the open Millennium problem) - what is the conceptual gap?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) For suitable weak solutions on R^3 x (0, T), the singular set has 1-dimensional parabolic Hausdorff measure zero; (b) suitable weak solution = Leray-Hopf weak solution that additionally satisfies a local energy inequality with the pressure; (c) global regularity asks whether the singular set is empty; CKN bounds its size but does not show emptiness. The open Millennium problem is closing this gap.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is the partial-regularity statement; multi-part adds suitable-weak definition + gap to Millennium problem
entries.source: Caffarelli-Kohn-Nirenberg 1982 (Communications on Pure and Applied Mathematics)

entries.single_part_probe: What does Kempe-Kitaev-Regev 2006 prove about Local Hamiltonian?
entries.single_part_expected: 2-Local Hamiltonian is QMA-complete.
entries.multi_part_probe: About QMA-completeness of Local Hamiltonian: (a) what locality threshold did Kitaev originally establish, and what did KKR improve to? (b) is QMA-completeness preserved on a 2D lattice? (c) does this give a quantum PCP-style constant gap?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Kitaev showed 5-Local Hamiltonian is QMA-complete (~1999/2002); KKR 2006 improved to 2-Local; (b) yes — Oliveira-Terhal 2008 showed 2-Local Hamiltonian on a 2D square lattice is QMA-complete; (c) no — the gap in these QMA-completeness results is inverse-polynomial, not constant; constant-gap QMA-completeness is the (open) quantum PCP conjecture
entries.decomposition_target: tests separation of locality threshold history (a), geometric refinement (b), and the gap-strength caveat that distinguishes QMA-completeness from qPCP (c)
entries.source: Kitaev-Shen-Vyalyi 2002 book; Kempe-Kitaev-Regev 2006 SIAM J. Comput.; Oliveira-Terhal 2008

entries.single_part_probe: State the Erdős-Ko-Rado theorem for k-element intersecting families on [n].
entries.single_part_expected: For n >= 2k, an intersecting family of k-subsets of [n] has size at most C(n-1, k-1).
entries.multi_part_probe: For k-element intersecting families on [n]: (a) state the EKR maximum bound; (b) describe the extremal families; (c) what changes when n < 2k?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) C(n-1, k-1) for n >= 2k; (b) extremal families are 'stars' — all k-sets containing a fixed element; for n > 2k stars are the unique extremal families up to relabeling; (c) for n < 2k every pair of k-subsets intersects automatically (pigeonhole), so the bound becomes trivial — all k-subsets form an intersecting family.
entries.decomposition_target: decomposes naturally into bound / structure / regime split
entries.source: Erdős, Ko, Rado 1961 (Quart. J. Math. Oxford 12)

entries.single_part_probe: Define the hyperarithmetic hierarchy and state its connection to Δ¹_1 sets.
entries.single_part_expected: The hyperarithmetic hierarchy is defined by transfinite iteration of the Turing jump along recursive ordinals: H_0 = 0, H_{a+1} = H_a', H_a = ⋂_{b<_O a} H_b along Kleene's O; a real x is hyperarithmetic iff x ≤_T H_a for some recursive ordinal a; by the Kleene–Suslin theorem, the hyperarithmetic sets coincide with the Δ¹_1 sets of reals
entries.multi_part_probe: Concerning the hyperarithmetic hierarchy: (a) define it and state its connection to Δ¹_1; (b) what is ω₁ᶜᵏ, and how does it relate to the hierarchy? (c) what does the Spector–Gandy theorem say about Π¹_1 sets and hyperarithmetic reductions?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) the hyperarithmetic hierarchy is defined by transfinite iteration of the Turing jump along recursive ordinals: H_0 = 0, H_{a+1} = H_a', H_a = ⋂_{b<_O a} H_b along Kleene's O; a real x is hyperarithmetic iff x ≤_T H_a for some recursive ordinal a; by the Kleene–Suslin theorem, the hyperarithmetic sets coincide with the Δ¹_1 sets of reals; (b) ω₁ᶜᵏ is the supremum of the recursive ordinals (the Church–Kleene ordinal); the hyperarithmetic hierarchy is exactly indexed by ordinals < ω₁ᶜᵏ; (c) the Spector–Gandy theorem states that Π¹_1 = Σ_1^{L_{ω₁ᶜᵏ}} (Π¹_1 sets are exactly those Σ_1-definable over the smallest admissible set L_{ω₁ᶜᵏ}), which gives a Levy-style normal form and connects Π¹_1 to admissible set theory / KP
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
entries.source: Kleene 1955 (Trans. AMS 79); Spector 1955 (J. Symb. Logic 20); Gandy 1960; Sacks, Higher Recursion Theory (1990)

entries.single_part_probe: State the Stein-Tomas restriction theorem for the sphere S^{n-1} in R^n.
entries.single_part_expected: The extension operator E: L^2(S^{n-1}) -> L^q(R^n), E(g)(x) = integral_{S^{n-1}} g(xi) e^{2pi i x.xi} dsigma(xi), is bounded for q >= 2(n+1)/(n-1).
entries.multi_part_probe: For the Fourier extension operator on S^{n-1}: (a) state Stein-Tomas; (b) what is the conjectured sharp range (Stein conjecture); (c) why does the Stein-Tomas L^2-orthogonality argument fail to reach the conjectured sharp range and what category of techniques pushes past it?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) E: L^2 -> L^q bounded for q >= 2(n+1)/(n-1); (b) Stein conjecture: E: L^q(S^{n-1}) -> L^q(R^n) bounded for q > 2n/(n-1); (c) Stein-Tomas uses T*T = convolution with the surface measure's Fourier transform which decays like |x|^{-(n-1)/2}; this bounds L^2-to-L^q only on the conjugate range tied to that decay rate. To push past requires bilinear/multilinear methods (Tao-Vargas-Vega, Bennett-Carbery-Tao), polynomial method (Guth), or l^2-decoupling (Bourgain-Demeter).
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is Stein-Tomas endpoint; multi-part adds conjecture + technique-category
entries.source: Tomas 1975 (Bull. AMS); Stein 1979 (conjecture in Beijing lectures, paraphrase on venue); Bourgain-Demeter 2015 (Annals)

entries.single_part_probe: State the Plancherel theorem for the Fourier transform on R^n.
entries.single_part_expected: The Fourier transform extends to a unitary operator on L^2(R^n); ||hat{f}||_{L^2} = ||f||_{L^2}.
entries.multi_part_probe: For the Fourier transform on R^n: (a) state Plancherel; (b) state Hausdorff-Young for 1 <= p <= 2; (c) describe what fails for p > 2 and why interpolation between L^1->L^infinity and L^2->L^2 gives the L^p->L^{p'} bound only on this range.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) ||hat{f}||_{L^2} = ||f||_{L^2}, FT is unitary on L^2; (b) for 1 <= p <= 2, FT maps L^p to L^{p'} with ||hat{f}||_{p'} <= ||f||_p (sharp constants Beckner 1975); (c) for p > 2, FT is not bounded L^p -> L^{p'} on R^n; the L^1 -> L^infinity and L^2 -> L^2 endpoints by Riesz-Thorin only give the convexity range 1 <= p <= 2.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is the Plancherel statement; multi-part folds in Hausdorff-Young + interpolation reasoning
entries.source: Plancherel 1910; Hausdorff 1923; Young 1912; sharp constants Beckner 1975 (Annals)

entries.single_part_probe: State the Calderon-Zygmund decomposition.
entries.single_part_expected: Given f in L^1(R^n) and lambda > 0, R^n decomposes as G union B where on G, |f| <= lambda a.e., and B is a disjoint union of dyadic cubes Q_j with average (1/|Q_j|) integral_{Q_j} |f| in (lambda, 2^n lambda] and total measure |B| <= ||f||_{L^1} / lambda.
entries.multi_part_probe: For the Calderon-Zygmund machinery on R^n: (a) state the Calderon-Zygmund decomposition; (b) describe how it implies the weak-type (1,1) estimate for a Calderon-Zygmund singular integral T (i.e., one whose kernel satisfies the Hormander integral condition); (c) what is the L^p analogue (1 < p < infinity) and what interpolation step uses (b)?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) standard CZ decomposition statement (see single_part_expected); (b) for a CZ singular integral T (a priori L^2-bounded with kernel K satisfying Hormander), one writes f = g + b with g good (L^2-controlled) and b = sum b_j bad atoms; T(g) is bounded by L^2-arguments; T(b_j) is concentrated near 2 Q_j and the Hormander condition gives the off-diagonal decay; one obtains |{|Tf| > lambda}| <= (C/lambda) ||f||_{L^1}; (c) Marcinkiewicz interpolation between the weak-(1,1) of (b) and L^2-boundedness gives L^p for 1 < p <= 2; duality (T*) gives 2 <= p < infinity.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is the decomposition; multi-part adds the singular-integral application + interpolation
entries.source: Calderon-Zygmund 1952 (Acta Mathematica)

entries.single_part_probe: State the K-method real interpolation construction (Peetre).
entries.single_part_expected: For a compatible Banach pair (A_0, A_1) and theta in (0,1), q in [1, infinity], the K-functional K(t, a) := inf{||a_0||_{A_0} + t ||a_1||_{A_1} : a = a_0 + a_1} defines the real interpolation space (A_0, A_1)_{theta, q} via ||a||_{theta,q} = (integral_0^infinity (t^{-theta} K(t, a))^q dt/t)^{1/q}.
entries.multi_part_probe: For real interpolation by the K-method: (a) define the K-functional and the interpolation space (A_0, A_1)_{theta, q}; (b) compare to the J-method (dual K-method) - what is the J-functional and how does it produce the same interpolation spaces; (c) give the standard identification when (A_0, A_1) = (L^{p_0}, L^{p_1}): which Lorentz space is (L^{p_0}, L^{p_1})_{theta, q}?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) K-functional and (A_0, A_1)_{theta, q} as in single_part_expected; (b) J-functional J(t, a) := max(||a||_{A_0}, t ||a||_{A_1}); the J-method represents elements as integrals of A_0 cap A_1 elements with weights, and gives the same interpolation space; the K-method and J-method are equivalent (with comparable norms), often stated as the K=J theorem in real interpolation; (c) (L^{p_0}, L^{p_1})_{theta, q} = L^{p_theta, q} where 1/p_theta = (1-theta)/p_0 + theta/p_1; this is the Lorentz space at exponent (p_theta, q).
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is K-method definition; multi-part adds J-method + Lorentz identification
entries.source: Peetre 1963; Bergh-Lofstrom 'Interpolation Spaces' (Springer Grundlehren 223, 1976)

entries.single_part_probe: What is the projective hierarchy on a Polish space, and what is the lightface vs. boldface distinction?
entries.single_part_expected: The projective hierarchy is built from analytic (Σ¹_1) sets by alternating projection and complement: Π¹_n = complements of Σ¹_n, Σ¹_{n+1} = projections of Π¹_n; lightface (Σ¹_n with no superscript) requires definability without parameters / by recursive parameters, while boldface (Σ¹_n) allows arbitrary real parameters
entries.multi_part_probe: Concerning the projective hierarchy: (a) define the hierarchy and state the lightface/boldface distinction; (b) what is the regularity-property status of Σ¹_2 sets in ZFC? (c) what large-cardinal hypothesis implies that all projective sets are Lebesgue measurable, have the Baire property, and the perfect-set property?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) the projective hierarchy is built from analytic (Σ¹_1) sets by alternating projection and complement: Π¹_n = complements of Σ¹_n, Σ¹_{n+1} = projections of Π¹_n; lightface (Σ¹_n with no superscript) requires definability without parameters / by recursive parameters, while boldface (Σ¹_n) allows arbitrary real parameters; (b) regularity for Σ¹_2 (Lebesgue measurability, BP, PSP) is independent of ZFC — e.g., V = L gives a non-measurable Σ¹_2 set (Gödel); under inaccessibles or stronger, all Σ¹_2 sets are regular (Solovay); (c) the existence of infinitely many Woodin cardinals (more precisely, projective determinacy, which follows from ω Woodin cardinals with a measurable above by Martin–Steel–Woodin) implies that all projective sets are Lebesgue measurable, have the Baire property, and the perfect-set property
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
entries.source: Moschovakis, Descriptive Set Theory (2nd ed.); Martin–Steel 1989 (JAMS 2); Woodin 1988 (PNAS 85)

entries.single_part_probe: State the Strichartz estimate for the linear Schrodinger equation on R^n.
entries.single_part_expected: For the linear Schrodinger equation i u_t + Delta u = 0 with u(0) = f, ||u||_{L^q_t L^r_x(R x R^n)} <= C ||f||_{L^2(R^n)} for any (q, r) admissible: 2/q + n/r = n/2 with 2 <= q, r <= infinity, and (q, r, n) != (2, infinity, 2).
entries.multi_part_probe: For the linear Schrodinger equation i u_t + Delta u = 0 on R^n: (a) state the Strichartz estimate (admissibility relation); (b) state the Keel-Tao endpoint Strichartz inequality and what the excluded triple is; (c) explain how the dual estimate gives an inhomogeneous Strichartz bound for the forced equation i u_t + Delta u = F.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Strichartz: ||u||_{L^q_t L^r_x} <= C ||u(0)||_{L^2} for admissible (q,r): 2/q + n/r = n/2, 2 <= q, r <= infinity; (b) Keel-Tao 1998 (American Journal of Mathematics) settled the endpoint case (q, r) = (2, 2n/(n-2)) for n >= 3; for n = 2 the endpoint (q,r) = (2, infinity) is FALSE (Tao 2000 counterexample for the 2D Schrodinger endpoint) - this is the (2,infinity,2) triple excluded above; (c) by the TT* argument, the dual Strichartz gives ||integral_0^t e^{i(t-s)Delta} F(s) ds||_{L^q_t L^r_x} <= C ||F||_{L^{q'}_t L^{r'}_x} for (q,r), (q',r') admissible.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is Strichartz; multi-part adds Keel-Tao endpoint + inhomogeneous form
entries.source: Strichartz 1977 (Duke Mathematical Journal); Keel-Tao 1998 (American Journal of Mathematics)

entries.single_part_probe: State Weyl's law for the asymptotic distribution of eigenvalues of the Dirichlet Laplacian on a bounded domain in R^n.
entries.single_part_expected: N(lambda) ~ (omega_n / (2pi)^n) * Vol(Omega) * lambda^{n/2} as lambda -> infinity, where omega_n is the volume of the unit ball in R^n and N(lambda) is the eigenvalue counting function.
entries.multi_part_probe: For the Dirichlet Laplacian on a bounded domain Omega in R^n: (a) state Weyl's law for the leading order of the eigenvalue counting function N(lambda); (b) state the second-order correction (Weyl conjecture) involving boundary surface area; (c) for which classes of domains is the second-order correction proven, and where is the sharp form open?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) N(lambda) ~ (omega_n / (2pi)^n) Vol(Omega) lambda^{n/2}; (b) Weyl conjecture: N(lambda) = (omega_n/(2pi)^n) Vol(Omega) lambda^{n/2} - (1/4) (omega_{n-1}/(2pi)^{n-1}) Vol(partial Omega) lambda^{(n-1)/2} + o(lambda^{(n-1)/2}); (c) proven by Ivrii 1980 under a non-self-focusing geodesic-billiard hypothesis (most domains satisfy this); the precise hypothesis under which the o-term is sharp remains a topic of refinement.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is leading order; multi-part folds in second-order correction + status
entries.source: Weyl 1911 (leading order); Weyl 1913 (conjecture); Ivrii 1980 (second-order, modulo billiard hypothesis)

entries.single_part_probe: State Weyl's equidistribution criterion for a sequence (x_n) in [0,1).
entries.single_part_expected: (x_n) is equidistributed mod 1 iff for every nonzero integer k, (1/N) sum_{n=1}^N exp(2 pi i k x_n) -> 0 as N -> infinity.
entries.multi_part_probe: For equidistribution of sequences mod 1: (a) state Weyl's equidistribution criterion via exponential sums; (b) use it to prove (n alpha) is equidistributed mod 1 for irrational alpha; (c) state Weyl's polynomial generalization: (P(n)) is equidistributed mod 1 for any real polynomial P with at least one irrational non-constant coefficient.

entries.single_part_probe: State Birkhoff's normal form theorem near an elliptic fixed point of a smooth area-preserving map of the plane.
entries.single_part_expected: Near an elliptic fixed point with eigenvalues e^{+/- 2 pi i alpha} where alpha is irrational and not a root of any low-order resonance, there exist symplectic coordinates (I, theta) in which the map takes the formal normal form (I, theta) -> (I, theta + omega(I)) up to flat-in-I error, where omega(I) = alpha + c_1 I + c_2 I^2 + ... is a formal power series.
entries.multi_part_probe: For an area-preserving smooth map F of R^2 with elliptic fixed point at the origin and rotation number alpha at the origin: (a) state Birkhoff normal form near the fixed point; (b) state Moser's twist theorem giving conditions under which invariant circles persist for the actual map (not just the normal form); (c) explain why Birkhoff normal form is generally divergent and what KAM provides on top.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Assuming alpha is non-resonant (k alpha not in Z for 1 <= |k| <= 2N), there exist symplectic coordinates (I, theta) in which F = R_omega + O(I^N) with omega(I) = alpha + c_1 I + ... + c_{N-1} I^{N-1}; (b) Moser's twist theorem (1962): if the map is C^k for k large enough (originally k = 333; subsequently lowered) and the twist coefficient c_1 != 0 (twist condition), then for every Diophantine omega in a Cantor set near alpha, there is an invariant circle of F with rotation number omega; (c) the Birkhoff normalization is a formal series in I, generally divergent because the small-divisors k alpha - m grow like factorials in the formal Taylor coefficients; KAM bypasses divergence by working on a Cantor-like set of Diophantine frequencies and using a Newton (quadratic) iteration that tolerates the Diophantine loss while preserving analyticity.
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer when the model can answer single-part correctly
entries.source: Birkhoff, 'Dynamical Systems' (1927); Moser, Nachr. Akad. Wiss. Goettingen (1962); Russmann, Regul. Chaotic Dyn. 6 (2001)

entries.single_part_probe: State the prime number theorem in its simplest form.
entries.single_part_expected: pi(x) ~ x / log x as x -> infinity, where pi(x) counts primes <= x.
entries.multi_part_probe: For the distribution of primes: (a) state the prime number theorem; (b) describe how the PNT follows from non-vanishing of zeta(s) on the line Re(s) = 1; (c) what is the Riemann-von Mangoldt explicit formula relating pi(x) to the zeros of zeta?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) pi(x) ~ x/log x or equivalently pi(x) ~ Li(x) := integral_2^x dt/log t; (b) the PNT is equivalent to the non-vanishing of zeta on Re(s) = 1: zeta(1 + it) != 0 for all real t; this uses the Hadamard / de la Vallee Poussin argument that 3 + 4 cos theta + cos 2 theta >= 0 to control |zeta(s)|^3 |zeta(s+it)|^4 |zeta(s+2it)| from below as s -> 1+ via Mertens-type identities; (c) Riemann-von Mangoldt explicit formula: psi(x) = x - sum_rho x^rho/rho - log(2 pi) - (1/2) log(1 - x^{-2}), where rho ranges over non-trivial zeta zeros and psi(x) = sum_{p^k <= x} log p; this exhibits PNT as the leading order x and quantifies the error term in terms of distance from the critical line.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is PNT; multi-part adds Re(s)=1 nonvanishing + explicit formula
entries.source: Hadamard 1896; de la Vallee Poussin 1896; Riemann 1859 (explicit formula); von Mangoldt 1895

entries.single_part_probe: Define the Mitchell order on normal measures.
entries.single_part_expected: For normal measures U, U' on κ, U ⊲ U' iff U ∈ Ult(V, U'); the relation ⊲ is well-founded, and its rank function o(U) gives a well-founded ordering on the normal measures over κ
entries.multi_part_probe: Concerning the Mitchell order on normal measures: (a) define the Mitchell order; (b) why is it well-founded? (c) what is the consistency strength of o(κ) = κ⁺⁺, and how does it relate to SCH?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) for normal measures U, U' on κ, U ⊲ U' iff U ∈ Ult(V, U'); the relation ⊲ is well-founded, and its rank function o(U) gives a well-founded ordering on the normal measures over κ; (b) Mitchell proved ⊲ is well-founded by an absoluteness/rank-comparison argument over iterated ultrapowers — if it had a descending chain, one could extract a non-well-founded iteration; (c) o(κ) = κ⁺⁺ is exactly the consistency strength of the failure of SCH at a singular limit (Gitik–Mitchell 1996), giving the optimal lower bound matching Magidor’s upper bound

entries.single_part_probe: State the Boltzmann-Grad limit and what equation it produces in the mean-field / kinetic regime for a hard-sphere system.
entries.single_part_expected: Letting N -> infinity and the diameter epsilon -> 0 with N epsilon^{d-1} -> constant (the Boltzmann-Grad scaling), the empirical one-particle distribution of a hard-sphere system converges (Lanford 1975, on a short time interval) to a solution of the Boltzmann equation: partial_t f + v . nabla_x f = Q(f, f), with Q the binary collision operator.
entries.multi_part_probe: For the derivation of kinetic equations from particle systems: (a) state the Boltzmann-Grad scaling and the resulting Boltzmann equation, citing Lanford's theorem; (b) state the regime (mean-field scaling N -> infinity, interaction strength 1/N, fixed range) producing the Vlasov equation instead, citing Braun-Hepp / Dobrushin; (c) explain Boltzmann's H-theorem and the entropy dissipation it gives.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Boltzmann-Grad scaling: N hard spheres of diameter epsilon in dimension d with N epsilon^{d-1} = constant, and N -> infinity, epsilon -> 0; Lanford (1975) proved that on a short time interval [0, T_0], the one-particle marginal of the BBGKY hierarchy converges to a solution f(t,x,v) of the Boltzmann equation partial_t f + v . nabla_x f = Q(f, f) with the standard hard-sphere collision kernel; (b) mean-field scaling: N particles with smooth pair interactions of strength 1/N and fixed range, N -> infinity; Braun-Hepp (1977) and Dobrushin (1979) proved convergence to the Vlasov equation partial_t f + v . nabla_x f + F[f] . nabla_v f = 0, where F[f] = - integral nabla phi(x - y) f(t, y, w) dy dw is the self-consistent mean-field force; (c) Boltzmann's H-theorem: the H-functional H(f) = integral f log f dx dv is non-increasing along solutions of the Boltzmann equation, with d/dt H(f) <= 0 and equality iff f is locally Maxwellian; this provides entropy dissipation and a Lyapunov functional driving relaxation toward equilibrium.

entries.source: Lanford, in 'Dynamical Systems, Theory and Applications', Springer LNP 38 (1975); Braun and Hepp, Comm. Math. Phys. 56 (1977); Dobrushin, Funct. Anal. Appl. 13 (1979); Boltzmann, Wiener Berichte (1872)

entries.single_part_probe: What is the rotation number of an orientation-preserving homeomorphism f: S^1 -> S^1?
entries.single_part_expected: rho(f) = lim_{n -> infinity} (F^n(x) - x) / n mod 1, where F: R -> R is any lift of f to the universal cover; the limit exists, is independent of x, and is well-defined mod 1.
entries.multi_part_probe: For the rotation number of an orientation-preserving circle homeomorphism f: (a) define the rotation number rho(f) and state independence of x; (b) state Poincare's classification: rho(f) is rational iff f has a periodic orbit (and in that case all periodic orbits have the same period q with rho(f) = p/q); (c) state Denjoy's theorem on conjugacy to a rotation when f is C^2 and rho(f) is irrational, and describe the C^1 counterexamples (Denjoy examples).
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Lift f to F: R -> R with F(x+1) = F(x) + 1; rho(f) = lim_n (F^n(x) - x)/n exists for every x and is independent of x and of choice of lift mod 1, by a sub-additivity argument due to Poincare; (b) Poincare (1885): rho(f) = p/q in lowest terms iff f has a periodic point of period q; in that case every periodic point has period q and they are cyclically ordered as the orbit of a rigid rotation by p/q; if rho(f) is irrational, f has no periodic points and is semi-conjugate to the rigid rotation R_{rho(f)}; (c) Denjoy (1932): if f is C^2 (or C^1 with derivative of bounded variation) and rho(f) is irrational, the semi-conjugacy is a conjugacy, so f is topologically conjugate to R_{rho(f)}; Denjoy's C^1 counterexamples (with continuous derivative but unbounded variation) are minimal C^1 diffeomorphisms with irrational rotation number whose orbits are dense only in a Cantor set, showing the C^2 hypothesis cannot be relaxed to C^1.
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer when the model can answer single-part correctly
entries.source: Poincare, J. Math. Pures Appl. (4) 1 (1885); Denjoy, J. Math. Pures Appl. (9) 11 (1932); Katok-Hasselblatt, 'Introduction to the Modern Theory of Dynamical Systems' Ch. 11-12

entries.single_part_probe: Define treewidth.
entries.single_part_expected: The treewidth of a graph G is the minimum, over all tree decompositions of G, of (max bag size - 1).
entries.multi_part_probe: Treewidth: (a) define treewidth via tree decompositions; (b) name two graph classes with bounded treewidth; (c) state the Courcelle theorem on monadic-second-order logic.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) a tree decomposition of G is a tree T whose nodes are labeled with subsets ('bags') of V(G) such that (i) every vertex is in some bag, (ii) for every edge uv, some bag contains both u and v, (iii) for every vertex v, the bags containing v form a connected subtree of T; treewidth tw(G) is min over decompositions of (max bag size - 1); (b) trees have treewidth 1; series-parallel graphs have treewidth <= 2; outerplanar graphs have treewidth <= 2; planar graphs do NOT have bounded treewidth; (c) Courcelle 1990: any graph property expressible in monadic-second-order logic (MSO) is decidable in linear time on graphs of bounded treewidth — a meta-algorithmic theorem covering many NP-hard problems on tree-like structures.
entries.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface Courcelle attribution + MSO-decidability framing
entries.source: Robertson-Seymour 1984 for treewidth definition; Courcelle 1990 (Inform. and Comput. 85)

entries.single_part_probe: State Ratner's measure classification theorem for unipotent flows on G / Gamma.
entries.single_part_expected: Let G be a connected Lie group, Gamma a lattice in G, and U a one-parameter Ad-unipotent subgroup acting on G / Gamma by translation. Every U-invariant ergodic Borel probability measure on G / Gamma is the natural Haar-type measure on a closed orbit of some closed subgroup H with U subset H subset G.
entries.multi_part_probe: For Ratner's theorems on unipotent flows: (a) state Ratner's measure classification theorem for U-invariant ergodic probability measures on G / Gamma; (b) state Ratner's orbit closure theorem (every U-orbit closure is homogeneous); (c) describe one application to equidistribution, e.g., the Dani-Margulis theorem on equidistribution of unipotent orbits or applications to the Oppenheim conjecture.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Every U-invariant ergodic probability measure on G / Gamma is the H-invariant Haar measure on a closed orbit Hx_0 / (Hx_0 cap Gamma) for some closed subgroup H with U subset H subset G; (b) for each x in G / Gamma the closure of the U-orbit { u_t x : t in R } is the orbit Hx of some closed subgroup H containing U, and Hx supports an H-invariant probability measure; (c) Dani-Margulis: for any x and any continuous compactly supported f on G / Gamma, (1/T) integral_0^T f(u_t x) dt converges to the integral against the Haar measure of the orbit closure Hx; this resolved the Oppenheim conjecture (Margulis 1989): every nondegenerate indefinite irrational quadratic form Q in n >= 3 variables has Q(Z^n) dense in R, by reducing to the orbit-closure structure of SO(2,1) on SL_3(R) / SL_3(Z).

entries.source: Ratner, Ann. of Math. 134 (1991); Ratner, Duke Math. J. 63 (1991); Margulis, in 'Dynamical Systems and Ergodic Theory' (Warsaw 1986); Dani and Margulis, Proc. Indian Acad. Sci. 101 (1991)

entries.single_part_probe: What was Keevash's 2014 breakthrough on combinatorial designs?
entries.single_part_expected: Keevash proved the existence conjecture for Steiner systems and more general t-designs whenever the natural divisibility conditions are satisfied (and n is sufficiently large).
entries.multi_part_probe: Keevash's existence-of-designs result: (a) state the result; (b) what was the prior status before 2014? (c) name a follow-up that gave a different proof.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) for fixed t, k and divisibility-satisfying n sufficiently large, there exists a Steiner system S(t, k, n) — and more generally Keevash's randomized algebraic construction proves existence of designs with the natural divisibility conditions; (b) prior to 2014, existence was known only for restricted (t,k) (Wilson's theorem for k=3 and graph designs; t=2 case largely resolved); the t-design existence question for t >= 3 had been open since Steiner 1853; (c) Glock, Kühn, Lo, Osthus (2016, arxiv 1611.06827) gave a purely combinatorial proof via iterative absorption.
entries.decomposition_target: wrapper must distinguish randomized-algebraic (Keevash) from absorption (GKLO) approach

entries.single_part_probe: State the conclusion of the Birkhoff pointwise ergodic theorem for an ergodic measure-preserving transformation T on a probability space (X, B, mu) with f in L^1(mu).
entries.single_part_expected: For mu-a.e. x, (1/n) sum_{k=0}^{n-1} f(T^k x) converges to integral f dmu as n -> infinity.
entries.multi_part_probe: For an ergodic measure-preserving T on (X, B, mu) with f in L^1(mu): (a) state the conclusion of the Birkhoff pointwise ergodic theorem; (b) explain how the maximal ergodic theorem (sup_n (1/n) sum f(T^k x) > lambda has measure at most ||f||_1 / lambda on the level set) is used in the proof; (c) describe how the conclusion fails for a non-ergodic T and what replaces the limit (the conditional expectation E[f | I] with respect to the sigma-algebra I of T-invariant sets).
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) For mu-a.e. x, (1/n) sum_{k=0}^{n-1} f(T^k x) -> integral f dmu; (b) the maximal inequality controls the size of the set where the sup of partial averages exceeds lambda, giving a weak-(1,1) bound that lets one upgrade L^2 mean convergence (von Neumann) to a.e. convergence by a standard density+truncation argument; (c) without ergodicity the limit is the conditional expectation E[f | I] of f given the invariant sigma-algebra I, which is constant a.e. iff T is ergodic.

entries.source: Birkhoff, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 17 (1931); Walters, 'An Introduction to Ergodic Theory' (1982) Ch. 1

entries.single_part_probe: State the Hales-Jewett theorem.
entries.single_part_expected: For all r, k there exists N such that any r-coloring of [k]^N contains a monochromatic combinatorial line.
entries.multi_part_probe: Hales-Jewett theorem: (a) state it; (b) what is the density version due to Furstenberg-Katznelson; (c) what does it imply about Szemerédi-type density results?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Hales-Jewett 1963: for all r, k there exists HJ(r,k) such that any r-coloring of the cube [k]^N (N >= HJ(r,k)) contains a monochromatic combinatorial line; (b) Furstenberg-Katznelson 1991: density Hales-Jewett — for any delta > 0 and k there exists N(delta, k) such that any subset of [k]^N of density >= delta contains a combinatorial line; (c) DHJ implies Szemerédi's theorem (k-AP density theorem) directly via projection of [k]^N to integers; the Polymath 1 project (2010, with Tao et al.) gave a combinatorial proof of DHJ, replacing the original ergodic-theoretic argument.
entries.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface DHJ -> Szemerédi implication and Polymath-1 attribution

entries.single_part_probe: State the Shannon-McMillan-Breiman theorem for an ergodic measure-preserving system (X, B, mu, T) with finite generating partition P.
entries.single_part_expected: For mu-a.e. x, -(1/n) log mu(P^{(n)}(x)) -> h_mu(T, P) as n -> infinity, where P^{(n)} = P v T^{-1}P v ... v T^{-(n-1)}P and P^{(n)}(x) is the atom containing x.
entries.multi_part_probe: For an ergodic measure-preserving (X, B, mu, T) with finite partition P: (a) state the Shannon-McMillan-Breiman theorem; (b) state the Kolmogorov-Sinai theorem on entropy via generating partitions; (c) explain how SMB combined with KS gives an asymptotic equipartition: 'most n-cylinders have measure approximately exp(-n h_mu(T))' for a generator P.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) -(1/n) log mu(P^{(n)}(x)) -> h_mu(T, P) for mu-a.e. x, with mu-a.e. and L^1 convergence; (b) Kolmogorov-Sinai: h_mu(T) = h_mu(T, P) for any countable measurable generating partition P (one with v_{n in Z} T^{-n} P = B mod mu), so the entropy of the partition equals the system entropy; (c) for a generator P, atoms P^{(n)}(x) form an exhausting refinement; SMB says mu(P^{(n)}(x)) ~ exp(-n h_mu(T)) on a set of measure -> 1 (asymptotic equipartition), so the typical number of n-cylinders carrying most of the measure is about exp(n h_mu(T)).
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer when the model can answer single-part correctly

entries.single_part_probe: State the Kahn-Kalai expectation-threshold conjecture.
entries.single_part_expected: For any monotone increasing property F on a finite set, the threshold p_c(F) is at most O(p_E(F) * log(|critical-subgraph-count|)) times the expectation threshold, where the log factor was the conjectured tightness.
entries.multi_part_probe: Kahn-Kalai expectation-threshold conjecture: (a) state the conjecture; (b) what is its current status; (c) name one application of the result.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Kahn-Kalai (2007) conjectured that for every monotone increasing property F on the discrete cube, the actual threshold p_c(F) satisfies p_c(F) <= K * p_E(F) * log(L(F)) for an absolute constant K, where p_E is the expectation threshold and L(F) the size of the largest minimal element; (b) PROVEN by Park and Pham (2022, arxiv 2203.17207); short proofs followed (Tran-Vu 2023, arxiv 2303.02144); (c) implies the Shamir conjecture on perfect-matching thresholds in random hypergraphs and many threshold-tightness results in random combinatorial structures.
entries.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface the Park-Pham 2022 attribution
entries.source: Kahn-Kalai 2007 (Combin. Probab. Comput. 16); Park-Pham 2022 (arxiv 2203.17207)

entries.single_part_probe: What does SETH (Strong Exponential Time Hypothesis) state?
entries.single_part_expected: For every epsilon > 0, there exists k such that k-SAT cannot be solved in time O((2 - epsilon)^n).
entries.multi_part_probe: About SETH: (a) what is its formal statement? (b) what algorithmic conditional results does it imply? (c) is SETH stronger than P != NP?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Impagliazzo-Paturi 1999/2001: for every epsilon > 0 there exists k_epsilon such that k_epsilon-SAT requires time at least (2 - epsilon)^n; (b) under SETH, conditional fine-grained lower bounds: edit distance and orthogonal vectors require near-quadratic time, all-pairs shortest paths require near-cubic time, and many others; (c) yes — SETH implies P != NP (since SAT is in NP) but the converse is open; SETH is strictly stronger than ETH (Exponential Time Hypothesis)
entries.decomposition_target: tests separation of statement (a), downstream consequences (b), and relative strength (c)
entries.source: Impagliazzo-Paturi 1999/2001 J. CSS

entries.single_part_probe: State the Min-Max (Courant-Fischer) characterization of the k-th eigenvalue of the Dirichlet Laplacian.
entries.single_part_expected: lambda_k = min_{V_k} max_{u in V_k, u != 0} (||grad u||^2_{L^2} / ||u||^2_{L^2}), where V_k ranges over k-dimensional subspaces of H^1_0(Omega).
entries.multi_part_probe: For the eigenvalues of the Dirichlet Laplacian on a bounded domain Omega in R^n: (a) state the min-max characterization; (b) what monotonicity in the domain follows; (c) is there an analogous variational characterization for the Neumann Laplacian, and does the same domain monotonicity hold?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) standard Courant-Fischer statement (see single_part_expected); (b) Dirichlet eigenvalues are monotone-decreasing under domain inclusion: Omega1 subset Omega2 implies lambda_k^{Dir}(Omega1) >= lambda_k^{Dir}(Omega2); (c) Neumann eigenvalues have analogous min-max but the domain monotonicity REVERSES or fails generically: Neumann eigenvalues are not monotone under inclusion, except for specific symmetric configurations (Friedlander 1991: relates Dirichlet and Neumann eigenvalues by lambda_k^N <= lambda_k^D with strict inequality for k >= 2, but this is not domain monotonicity).
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is variational principle; multi-part adds monotonicity + Neumann distinction
entries.source: Courant-Hilbert 'Methods of Mathematical Physics' Vol. I (1924); Friedlander 1991 for Dirichlet/Neumann comparison

entries.single_part_probe: What does the Baker-Gill-Solovay 1975 theorem say?
entries.single_part_expected: There exist oracles A and B such that P^A = NP^A and P^B != NP^B.
entries.multi_part_probe: About relativization: (a) what did Baker-Gill-Solovay 1975 prove? (b) what does this imply about proof techniques for P vs NP? (c) name a non-relativizing proof technique.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) Baker-Gill-Solovay 1975 SIAM J. Comput.: there exist oracles A and B such that P^A = NP^A while P^B != NP^B; (b) any proof technique that relativizes (works the same with any oracle) cannot resolve P vs NP, since it would have to give a single answer in both relativized worlds; (c) arithmetization / sumcheck (used in IP=PSPACE by Shamir 1992) is non-relativizing — Fortnow-Sipser had constructed an oracle relative to which coNP is not contained in IP
entries.decomposition_target: tests separation of statement (a), implication for proof techniques (b), and concrete non-relativizing example (c)
entries.source: Baker-Gill-Solovay 1975 SIAM J. Comput.; Shamir 1992 J. ACM

entries.single_part_probe: What is the kneading sequence of a unimodal map and what does it determine?
entries.single_part_expected: The kneading sequence is the itinerary of the critical value c_1 = f(c) under f, encoded by a sequence in {L, C, R} according to whether f^n(c_1) is in the left interval, the critical point, or the right interval; it determines the topological conjugacy class of the unimodal map (Milnor-Thurston: two unimodal maps with same kneading sequence and no homtervals are topologically conjugate on their dynamical core).
entries.multi_part_probe: For unimodal interval maps and their kneading theory: (a) define the kneading sequence; (b) state Milnor-Thurston's theorem connecting kneading invariants and topological conjugacy; (c) describe the kneading determinant / zeta function and how it computes topological entropy.
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) For a unimodal f: I -> I with critical point c, the kneading sequence is the symbolic itinerary K(f) = (epsilon_1 epsilon_2 ...) of f(c), where epsilon_n in {L, C, R} according to whether f^n(c) lies left of c, equals c, or lies right of c (the C symbol terminates the sequence); (b) Milnor-Thurston (1988): two unimodal maps without 'homtervals' (intervals all of whose iterates avoid c) and the same kneading sequence are topologically conjugate on their non-wandering dynamics; the kneading sequence is therefore a complete combinatorial invariant for the dynamics; (c) the kneading determinant is a power series D(t) = sum theta_n t^n in Z[[t]] derived from K(f); Milnor-Thurston show that the smallest positive zero t_* of D(t) equals 1/exp(h_top(f)), so h_top(f) = -log t_* = log s where s is the growth rate of the lap-number sequence ell_n = #(monotone branches of f^n).
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers the multi-part answer when the model can answer single-part correctly
entries.source: Milnor and Thurston, in Dynamical Systems (Maryland 1986-87), Springer LNM 1342 (1988); de Melo and van Strien, 'One-Dimensional Dynamics' (1993) Ch. II

entries.single_part_probe: State the L^p boundedness of the Riesz transforms on R^n.
entries.single_part_expected: The Riesz transforms R_j f := c_n p.v. integral (x_j - y_j)/|x-y|^{n+1} f(y) dy (for j = 1, ..., n) are bounded on L^p(R^n) for 1 < p < infinity, with weak-type (1,1) and bounded on H^1 / BMO at the endpoints.
entries.multi_part_probe: For the Riesz transforms R_j on R^n: (a) state the L^p boundedness; (b) the Riesz transforms are the prototypical Calderon-Zygmund operators - state their kernel form and give one verification of the Hormander integral condition; (c) what is the role of the Riesz transforms in characterizing W^{1,p} regularity (relate || nabla f||_p and ||(-Delta)^{1/2} f||_p)?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) L^p-bounded for 1 < p < infinity; weak-(1,1) at p=1; H^1 -> L^1 and L^infinity -> BMO at p = infinity (Fefferman-Stein 1972 atomic decomposition); (b) kernel K_j(x) = c_n x_j / |x|^{n+1}; size bound |K_j(x)| <= C|x|^{-n}; gradient bound |grad K_j(x)| <= C|x|^{-(n+1)}; Hormander integral integral_{|x|>=2|y|} |K_j(x-y) - K_j(x)| dx <= C uniformly in y, by integrating the gradient bound across the strip; (c) the multiplier xi_j / |xi| of R_j satisfies sum_j xi_j R_j = (-Delta)^{1/2} (modulo constants); for nice f, ||grad f||_p ~ ||(-Delta)^{1/2} f||_p with constants depending only on p, n, by composition with R_j.
entries.decomposition_target: single-part is the boundedness theorem; multi-part adds kernel form + Sobolev characterization
entries.source: M. Riesz 1928 (Hilbert transform); Calderon-Zygmund 1952; Fefferman-Stein 1972 (Acta Mathematica) for H^1/BMO endpoint

entries.single_part_probe: State Woodin's HOD dichotomy theorem.
entries.single_part_expected: If κ is an extendible cardinal, then exactly one of the following holds: (i) every regular cardinal δ ≥ κ is measurable in HOD, or (ii) every singular cardinal γ > κ is singular in HOD and (γ⁺)^HOD = γ⁺ (the HOD Hypothesis holds at κ)
entries.multi_part_probe: Concerning Woodin's HOD dichotomy: (a) state the dichotomy theorem; (b) what is the HOD conjecture? (c) what is the present status: which side of the dichotomy is conjectured to hold under sufficient large cardinals, and is this proved?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) if κ is an extendible cardinal, then exactly one of the following holds: (i) every regular cardinal δ ≥ κ is measurable in HOD, or (ii) every singular cardinal γ > κ is singular in HOD and (γ⁺)^HOD = γ⁺ (the HOD Hypothesis holds at κ); (b) the HOD conjecture asserts that ZFC + 'there is an extendible cardinal' proves the HOD Hypothesis (option (ii)); (c) under sufficient large cardinals (extendibles), Woodin conjectures option (ii) holds (HOD is 'close' to V); the conjecture is open as of 2026; recent work by Goldberg has shed light on partial reductions but the full HOD conjecture remains unresolved
entries.decomposition_target: the paired structure validates whether E007 decomposition wrapper recovers (a)+(b)+(c) when the model can answer the single-part (a) correctly
entries.source: Woodin 2017 (Bull. Symb. Logic, In Search of Ultimate-L); Woodin–Davis–Rodríguez, 'The HOD Dichotomy' arXiv:1605.00613; Goldberg 2024 talks/notes on optimality of the HOD dichotomy

entries.single_part_probe: State the chromatic-threshold version of Turán's theorem (Erdős-Stone-Simonovits).
entries.single_part_expected: For any graph H with chromatic number chi(H) = r+1 >= 3, ex(n, H) = (1 - 1/r + o(1)) n^2 / 2.
entries.multi_part_probe: Erdős-Stone-Simonovits theorem: (a) state the asymptotic edge bound for an arbitrary graph H of chromatic number r+1; (b) why does the bound depend only on chromatic number? (c) what does it say (degenerate case) for bipartite H?
entries.multi_part_expected: (a) ex(n,H) = (1 - 1/r + o(1)) n^2/2 where chi(H) = r+1; (b) any H of chromatic number r+1 sits inside a sufficiently-blown-up balanced complete (r+1)-partite graph by Ramsey/blow-up; (c) for bipartite H (chi=2) the formula gives o(n^2) — it is asymptotically uninformative; sharper polynomial-exponent bounds (Kővári-Sós-Turán) take over.
entries.decomposition_target: tests decomposition recovery on degenerate-case caveat
entries.source: Erdős-Stone 1946; Erdős-Simonovits 1966
